DIDC (Defense Integrated Data Center): A data center under the Ministry of National Defense, with one center
in Yongin and two centers in Gyeryongdae.
Old KIATIS (Old KIATIS): The 625 Fallen Soldiers Comprehensive Information System. From 2007 until
May 2018, it was hosted within an internet homepage server and was later laundered to operate directly exposed
on the internet within an internet email server.
New KIATIS: The 625 Fallen Soldiers Comprehensive Information System Performance Improvement Project
ran from 2018 to 2022. Its operational deployment was delayed due to issues like speed problems and program
development delays.
VPN (Virtual Private Network): A virtual private network, it is a secure access method via the internet (or the
Defense Network).
Obstruction of Official Duties by Fraud: A crime defined in Article 137 of the Criminal Act, referring to acts
that obstruct the performance of duties by public officials through falsehoods or deception.
Defense Computing Information Agency (DCIA): The DCIA is responsible for performing project
management tasks according to a series of project management procedures and methods, such as business contract,
schedule management, risk management, configuration management, and quality management, from preparation
of the project order to the completion of the project. In the case of all-military support projects and information
systems operated by the Defense headquarters, the Defense Computing Information Service takes responsibility
for, and in the case of the rest of the institutional projects, the department in charge of project management in the
relevant unit/organization corresponds to the following tasks. Project preparation (project plan preparation,
proposal request form, support for drafting system specifications, etc.) and ordering Project
management activities (schedule management, risk management, configuration management, quality
management, etc.) Support for informatization strategy plan establishment Test and evaluation
support Project inspection SW acquisition qualification support (technical support such as
information system installation, data transfer, handover to the maintenance agency, etc.) (Duties of
Informatization Project-related Institutions Article 11 (4)).
MND Agency for KIA Recovery & Identification (MKIA): 「June 25th Comprehensive Information
System Performance Improvement Project (The New KIATIS Project Period '18.12.14 ~ '19.9.30)
requires 8 months from system analysis to implementation and 2 months for evaluation according to
the project plan prepared by the National Power of Korea with the acquiring agency. The budget is
calculated at around 670 million won based on the software project cost calculation guideline, which
is classified according to the project control agency as an MKIA delegated project (page 4), and the
system plan acquisition is carried out through method R&D and collective acquisition. It is described
(page 20), and the project management plan by the project promotion organization and mission is
“successful completion of the informatization project mission within the planned period/budget” for
the purpose of “successful completion of the informatization project mission”. The task of
coordinating/controlling major issues is specified by the National Powers of Korea as a project
management institution, such as hosting development/operation tests and evaluations (page 29).
Operational testing (OT) is described as being hosted by the project management organization (page

sections.title: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY (2 pages)
sections.body:
A Treatise Proven By A 7- Layer Verification System
Concerning The Cover-Up Of The 2016 Hacking Incident And Organized
Crime By The Ministry Of National Defense
And Its Affiliated Agencies
Subtitle: The Ministry of National Defense Military Prosecutor's Office, the Ministry of National
Defense, and its various organizations concealed the root server (Yongin) of the 2016 North
Korean hacking incident at the Defense Integrated Data Center, which was proven
according to the order of the seven-Layer verification system. To hide their own
mistakes, which they falsely covered up to the public and media,
they engaged in long-term, organized, and collective collusion,
concealment, manipulation, concealment, human rights
violations, and criminal stigmatization.

In 2016, North Korea hacked the Republic of Korea's Defense Integrated Data Center (DIDC)
Center 1 (Yongin). The actual intrusion route was an old system called the old KIATIS, which had been
installed and operated within the Defense Internet homepage server on the internet without VPN
protection for 15 years. To conceal this critical security breach, military prosecutors and defense IT
officials attempted to frame the author (an officer with 32 years of service) who discovered it in 2022
as a criminal. This thesis proves systematic criminality through a seven-layered proof system based on
digital evidence (official documents, contracts, transcripts) collected over four years.
The core finding is that the "source server" for the 2016 North Korean hack of the Defense Integrated
Data Center was the old KIATIS (625 War Dead Comprehensive Information System). This system
operated on the internet without VPN protection from 2007 to 2022, violating cybersecurity regulations
throughout this period. Exactly 48 hours after the author disclosed this fact at a meeting on February
2022, the defense intelligence cartel responded with a false report of abuse of authority.
This paper proves systematic crime through seven interconnected layers (a seven-layer proof system).
The first layer (2017-2024) shows how the cartel removed old KIATIS records and altered defense
regulations through the Active-X removal project. The second layer (2018-2022) documents the
manipulation of project approval documents, project plans, and requests for proposals. The third layer
(December 2018 - October 2019) proves the author's normal performance of duties as the head of
the project management team. The author did not participate in the test evaluation, and this role is not
subject to such participation. The fourth layer (December 2018 - September 2019) reveals
systematic manipulation during the test evaluation. The Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA)
manipulated research results to justify changes to defense regulations. The fifth layer (February 2022
- September 2022) proves the organized collusion behind a false power abuse report filed within 48
hours after the February 8 meeting. The sixth layer (April 2022–October 2024) shows the military
prosecution's fraudulent investigation, retroactively criminalizing normal operations from three years
prior. The seventh layer (September 2022–October 2022) traces the entire cartel network through
the submission of a petition and the halting of the fraudulent investigation.
The most decisive evidence is the transcript and the official document supporting it. The VPN and
database access control system which the military prosecutor claims the individual manipulated did not
exist during the Test Evaluation period or thereafter, from 27 August 2019 until at least 23 April 2021.
This document shows that the system was not used for at least 18 months. Ironically, the same document
was approved by Section Chief J.Y.I., one of the cartel's core members.
As a legal issue, the military prosecution argued that it violated the law by failing to notify the
evaluation committee members (21 individuals) that the test environment differed from the actual
operational environment. However, four facts refute this claim. First, the VPN and database access
control system did not exist during the relevant period. Second, the author was not a member of the
evaluation committee and could not have participated in the evaluation. Third, prosecutors retroactively
criminalized work successfully completed three years prior. Fourth, prosecutors selectively targeted
only the author, ignoring the document's final approver and creator.
This incident exhibits the structure of organized crime, demonstrating an APT-style targeted attack.
The attack demonstrated targeting (filing a false report within 48 hours of the facts being disclosed),
persistence (six months of isolation and retroactive criminalization), and sophistication (a five-stage
systematic attack: evidence destruction, social isolation, reputation terrorism, criminal stigmatization,
and building an intricate human network to block accountability tracing).
The implications of this case reveal a new form of organized, manipulated evil in the digital age.
Unlike Hannah Arendt's "banality of evil," which described a many-to-many structure in conventional
warfare, this case demonstrates a many-to-one structure targeting a single individual. The cartel
systematically destroyed one person using cybersecurity techniques learned in the digital age. This case
demonstrates how state power, under the guise of national security, can destroy an individual and
conceal the truth through collusion between military and judicial systems. It also proves that digital
evidence in the information age enables accountability for organized crime.

Evidence of Organized Crime to Cover Up the 2016
DIDC Hacking by Defense Organizations
by Youngju, LEE

sections.title: 1. Key Findings
sections.body:
In the 2016 North Korean hacking incident targeting the Defense Integrated Data Center (DIDC)
the actual point of entry was the Defense Internet Homepage server and the old KIATIS operations
running within it. This server was operated directly exposed to the internet without a VPN for 15 years,
from 2007 to 2022, by the Defense Computing Information (DCIA) and the Defense Integrated Data
Center 1 (Yongin). To conceal this critical security breach, the Ministry of National Defense Prosecutor's
Office and the defense IT cartel attempted to frame and eliminate me (Army Lieutenant Colonel Lee
Youngju, 32 years of service), who discovered this fact in 2022, as a fabricated criminal. This thesis
proves the full scope of this organized crime by state power through a seven-layered verification system,
based on digital evidence (official documents, transcripts, evidence records) collected over four years.

sections.title: 2. Background of the Incident and Cover-Up Process
sections.body:
Discovery of the Root Server: The root server behind the DIDC hacking incident revealed in this
thesis is the 625 Fallen Soldiers Comprehensive Information System (formerly KIATIS 8 ). It was
operated by the Defense Computing Information (DCIA) from 2007 to 2014, and subsequently by DIDC
Center 1 (Yongin). This operation was run as a parasite on part of the Defense Internet homepage server
and operated directly exposed to the internet without a VPN from 2007 to 2016. This constituted one of
the most severe security breaches since the founding of the military.
The Beginning of Concealment: Following the hacking incident in September 2016, the Ministry of
National Defense Prosecutor's Office announced the investigation results as "the work of a North Korean
hacker organization." However, the old KIATIS, which had been operated at DIDC Center 1 (Yongin)
– the actual point of intrusion – was concealed. Subsequently, starting in 2016, an organized cover-up
operation began to erase the very existence of the old KIATIS from records through the Active-X
removal project.
Trigger for Targeting: On February 2022, during a discussion on the new KIATIS improvement
direction that I chaired, a MKIA staff member mentioned "the fact that the old KIATIS had been
operated for 15 years without an internet remote VPN." Exactly 48 hours later, on February 2022, a

KIATIS is an acronym for Killed In Action Total Information System.
false report of abuse of authority was filed. Subsequently, I was isolated in a solitary cell for six months,
cut off from information, and subjected to an organized reputation attack.
Fabricated Deferred Prosecution: Investigation commenced on April 2022. On July the
Military Prosecutor's Office suddenly criminalized work I had performed normally three years prior, in
September 2019. The charge was " " (obstruction of official duties through deception), alleging I had
manipulated the test evaluation environment. However, decisive counterevidence exists in the form of
an official document. This document proves that the VPN and database access control system the
military prosecutors claimed was "manipulated" remained unused for one year and six months, from
August 2019, to April 2021. Even more ironic is the fact that this document was personally
approved by Section Chief J.Y.I., one of the key figures in the defense informatization cartel.
Result: On October 2022, I received a deferred prosecution decision on six charges. On October 2022, I had to end my 32-year military career in disgrace. During this process, my family was
destroyed, and I was socially ruined.

sections.title: 3. Seven-Layered Proof System
sections.body:
This thesis proves the crime through a systematic, seven-layered proof structure.
The First Layer (2017-2024) addresses the process of removing and concealing the history of the old
KIATIS, revising and manipulating directives during the Active-X removal project period. This proves
the organized cover-up that erased the very existence of the old KIATIS from the records.
The Second Layer (2018-2022) analyzes the process of manipulating the new KIATIS project
promotion system, project approvals, project proposals, and requests for proposals (RFP). This proves
a structure designed to evade responsibility by manipulating project documents.
The third layer (December 2018 - October 2019) proves the normal performance of duties
during the period when I, after being assigned to the DCIA, initiated, concluded, and oversaw the
management of the software development project. It specifically proves that I did not participate in the
test evaluation and was in a position where participation was impossible.
The fourth Layer (December 2018 - September 2019) addresses systematic manipulation,
concealment, conspiracy, and attempts to cover up the development and operational test evaluation of
the new KIATIS system. It specifically proves that the Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA)
manipulated the results of its test evaluation improvement study to justify the revision of the Ministry
of National Defense's directive.
The Fifth Layer (February 2022 - September 2022) addresses the forced solitary confinement and
character assassination following the false power abuse report. It specifically proves the organized
collusion behind the false power abuse report filed within 48 hours after the February 2022 meeting.
The sixth layer (April 2022–October 2024) analyzes the false entries, fabrications, concealments,
searches and seizures, and criminal stigmatization revealed during the military prosecution's targeted
investigation. This proves the selective and retaliatory fraudulent investigation that suddenly
transformed normal duties into crimes up until three years ago.
The Seventh Layer (September 2022 - October 2022) traces the entire process from the
submission of the petition and the demand for investigation to the eventual deferred prosecution. This
presents a comprehensive tracking map tracing the individuals, organizations, and timeline of the entire
cartel network.
Each layer constitutes an independent yet interconnected evidence system, collectively completing
the structure of a single massive, organized crime operation.

sections.title: 4. Decisive Evidence
sections.body:
The first and most decisive piece of evidence is an official document proving the non-use of a VPN.
This document explicitly states that the New KIATIS system did not utilize a VPN or database access
control system from August 2019, to April 2021. This proves that the system the military
prosecutor claims I "manipulated" did not exist at that time. More significantly, this official document
was personally approved by Section Chief J.Y.I., one of the key figures in the Defense
Informationization Cartel.
The second piece of evidence is a timeline that clearly reveals the causal relationship. The causal
relationship of the incident is clearly revealed through the chronological order. On February 2022,
during a discussion I presided over, I mentioned the fact of the old KIATIS's internet exposure. Exactly
48 hours later, on February a false report of abuse of authority occurred, and I was isolated in a
solitary confinement cell. On April 2022, the Ministry of National Defense's Inspector General
notified me of the "Audit Results and Disposition Request," concluding the false power abuse report.
Investigation commenced on April On July the military prosecution suddenly raised issues about
the three-year-old incident and conducted a search and seizure. Three months later, on October a
deferred prosecution disposition was issued, effectively forcing me into disgrace. This temporal
sequence is not coincidental but clear evidence of organized collusion.
The third piece of evidence is the record of evidence collected over four years. The individual
compiled evidence records from page 00001 to page 13,528 over four years. Based on conversations
with and evidence records from individuals including DCIA's Director P.H.G., Section Chief J.Y.I., the
Military Prosecutor General, prosecutors, and lawyers, a '7-Layed Proof System' was established,
structured across seven layers according to key phases and situations.9 In particular, the transcripts of
conversations with stakeholders contain vivid evidence of organized collusion, gaslighting, and
reputation terrorism.
The fourth piece of evidence is the test evaluation results approved by the Evaluation Committee on
September 2019. The development and operational test evaluation of the new KIATIS was
successfully concluded on September 2019, receiving a high score of 99.7 points and a determination
of military suitability by the Evaluation Committee. The Evaluation Committee approved it, and the
Director of the DCIA gave final approval. As the head of the project management team, I was in a
position that did not allow me to participate in the evaluation.

sections.title: 5. Legal Issues
sections.body:
The military prosecution claimed that "failure to notify the evaluation committee members that the
test evaluation environment differed from the actual operational environment constituted obstruction of
official duties through deception." However, my evidence-based rebuttal is as follows. First, the VPN
and database access control system that the military prosecution claimed was "manipulated" did not
exist at that time. This is clearly proven by official documents. Second, I was not an evaluation
committee member. The position of project management team leader is restricted from participating in
test evaluations. Third, suddenly defining a project successfully completed three years prior as a crime
demonstrates temporal arbitrariness. Fourth, Director Y.I.W., the final approver of the same official
document, and the project practitioner who drafted it were not investigated. Selectively making only me
a criminal is clear evidence of a targeted investigation. Additionally, over 400 pages of evidence
revealed the collective, organized conspiracy, cover-up, and unethical human rights violations of the
defense informatization cartel. Most importantly, it clearly demonstrated that the Ministry of National
Defense Prosecutor's Office conducted a "fraud investigation." You will witness the Ministry of National
Defense Prosecutor's Office as a criminal institution.

sections.title: 6. Structure of Organized Crime
sections.body:
The Ministry of National Defense and its affiliated organizations (hereafter referred to as the Defense
Informatization Cartel) launched an APT-style targeted attack against an individual. It may be hard to
believe, but it is true. This case exhibits the identical structure of an APT (Advanced Persistent Threat)style targeted attack as described in cybersecurity. The targeting (Threat) is evident from the false report
occurring within 48 hours after the February 2022, meeting.
Persistence is demonstrated by the six-month solitary confinement and the retroactive criminalization
of an incident from three years prior. Advanced is confirmed in the five-stage organized attack. These
stages are evidence destruction, social isolation, reputation terrorism, criminal stigmatization, and
building a sophisticated human network to block responsibility tracing. Members of the Defense
Information Cartel: Members of the Defense Informatization Cartel.: MND and Multiple Defense
Ministry affiliated agencies participated in this organized crime by dividing roles.
The Ministry of National Defense Prosecutors' Office conducted targeted investigations and
fabricated fraud investigations. The Defense Computing Information (DCIA) led evidence fabrication
and the isolation of the individual in solitary confinement. The Ministry of National Defense Information
Planning Office manipulated directives and project documents. DIDC manipulated the test environment.
The Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA) played a role in justifying the revision of the Ministry
of National Defense directives through the fabrication of research reports. Military Counterintelligence
Command (formerly Military Security Support Command (MSSC)) evaded security measures on the
old KIATIS system, which was undergoing performance upgrades. The Cyber Command appears to
have failed to conduct vulnerability assessments on the old KIATIS system for 15 years. The Ministry
of National Defense Investigation Headquarters, along with the DCIA, conducted a fraudulent
investigation into false reports of abuse of authority by users of the old KIATIS system. The Ministry
of National Defense Legal Affairs Office distributed false "warning letters.", conducted a fraudulent
investigation into false reports of abuse of authority by users of the old KIATIS system. The Ministry
of National Defense Legal Affairs Office distributed false "warning letters." The Minister of Defense
and the Defense Adviser tacitly accepted my report, whilst the Chief Prosecutor of the Ministry of
Defense avoided it. The lawyer (Hong Chang-sik, current Legal Affairs Manager of the Ministry of
National Defense), who is my lawyer, performed the role of the Ministry of National Defense
Prosecutor's Office. He defended the Ministry's claim regarding the 2016 North Korean hacking of the
Defense Integrated Data Center and attempted to discredit the messenger by portraying me as mentally
ill. The criminal acts of the defense organization were verified primarily through recordings, crossreferenced with official documents.9 Furthermore, this thesis reveals numerous shocking facts through
the words and actions of various individuals.

sections.title: 7. Social Significance
sections.body:
This paper thoroughly investigates a crime scene that goes beyond Hannah Arendt, revealing an
"organized and manipulated evil." It exposes the reality of heinous crimes from a perspective distinct
from the "banality of evil" presented in Hannah Arendt's Eichmann in Jerusalem. The "banality of evil"
Arendt focused on was a world-historical act of human atrocity occurring within the traditional war
structure of many against many. However, this case involves a specialized structure of many against
one.
It reveals the mechanism by which the collective complicity, adherence, and obedience of Defense
Informatization Cartel, individual public officials, military personnel, researchers, and corporate
personnel collectively create immense evil. Moreover, a distinct difference lies in the fact that the
criminal activities were deliberate, organized, and systematic over a long period, mediated by digital
information within or through the latest information technology (IT) and cyberspace. Above all, this
case will be the first in South Korean defense history where the new criminal form of the white-collar
"organized and manipulated devil" in the information society, along with the level and involvement in
such crimes, can be traced and held accountable based on digital evidence.
This study is a unique case of insider research based on primary data directly collected and analyzed
by the victim over four years. It holds significant academic value as it vividly reveals, through evidencebased insights, the covert criminal processes within a national institution that are difficult for external
researchers to access. This paper will be the first case in South Korean defense history to prove organized
crime based on evidence. Specifically, it proves through evidence the extreme atrocities committed by

This is why this thesis reached 400 pages. While one might blame the author's writing style, the author's writings over four
years far exceed 1,000 pages. Furthermore, various individuals participated directly or indirectly in this crime.
the MND And its affiliated agencies—acts beyond the capacity of ordinary humans. In the process of
this proof, it thoroughly dissects and demonstrates the fact that "the Ministry of National Defense
Prosecutors' Office is a criminal institution."

sections.title: 8. Conclusion
sections.body:
This paper is not merely an appeal for an individual's grievance. It is a modern-day example of
collusion between the defense and judicial systems, demonstrating how the abuse of state power under
the pretext of national security destroys an individual and conceals the truth. Digital evidence is a
double-edged sword. The head of the defense informatization cartel, the organization, and their cohorts
have likely been laughing all the way, celebrating the success of their brilliant cover-up operation. But
the digital traces they left behind have now become the blade turning against them.

The key terms and abbreviations necessary for understanding this argument are summarized as
follows.
First, terms related to organizations and institutions. DIDC stands for the Defense Integrated Data
Center, a data center under the Ministry of National Defense. It has one center in Yongin and two centers
in Gyeryongdae, tasked with information protection and cybersecurity for its cyber assets. The Ministry
of National Defense's Office of Information Planning oversees defense cyber security (protection)
policy. DCIA stands for Defense Computing Information Agency, which is a specialized project
management agency for the Ministry of National Defense's information technology projects. It conducts
security activities throughout the development lifecycle, including development and operational
support. MKIA stands for MND agency for KIA Recovery & Identification, which excavates the
remains of Korean War casualties and is the lead agency for the KIATIS project. The ROK Armed
Forces Counterintelligence Command, formerly the ROK Armed Forces Security Support Command,
is responsible for reviewing security measures and conducting evaluations. KIDA stands for the Korea
Institute for Defense Analyses, an institution researching defense policy. The Cyber Command conducts
cyber operations and performs regular and ad hoc vulnerability assessments. The Investigation
Headquarters is responsible for investigating criminal incidents and preventing crimes within the
defense cyber environment. The Investigation Headquarters is also a user member for KIATIS
operations.
Technical terms include the following. VPN stands for Virtual Private Network, meaning a virtual
private network, and is a secure access method via the internet. OTP stands for One-Time Password,
referring to a card that generates a one-time password for VPN access. DB Access Control System refers
to Shakra Max, a security system that controls database access. CS refers to the Client-Server model,
denoting direct communication between a client and a server. The Defense Network refers to the
military's internal computer network, the intranet. There are also terms related to projects and systems.
The old KIATIS was the 625 Killed In Action Total Information System. It operated directly exposed
to the internet at from 2007 to 2016 and continued to operate at least until 2022. New KIATIS (New
KIATIS) is the 625 Fallen Soldiers Comprehensive Information System Performance Improvement
Project, conducted from 2018 to October 2019. The Active-X Removal Project is the Defense Website
Standardization Project initiated in 2017.
Legal terms include the following: Obstruction of Official Duties by Fraud is defined in Article 137
of the Criminal Act, referring to the crime of obstructing a public official's duties through falsehood or
deception. The National Contract Act is the law governing contracts involving the state as a party,
regulating contracts and management for national projects. Deferred prosecution means acknowledging
criminal suspicion but not proceeding with prosecution. Terminology related to roles and positions also
requires clarification. The project management agency is the specialized institution responsible for
project commissioning and development management; the MND agency for KIA Recovery &
Identification (MKIA) fulfills this role. The project management team leader is ineligible to participate
in testing and evaluation. The project-managing agency is the institution that proposes project
requirements and actually executes and operates the system; the MKIA falls under this category and
oversees operational test and evaluation. The project-controlling agency is the institution that approves,
coordinates, and controls the project; the Ministry of National Defense's Information Planning Office
and the respective service branches' Information Planning Offices fall under this category. The
evaluation committee is the organization that conducts development and operational test and evaluation
(separately or integrated).
Keywords: APT-style targeted attacks, Defense Integrated Data Center (DIDC) Center 1 (Yongin) and
Center 2 (Gyeryongdae), development test evaluation, project management agency, project management
agency, project control agency, operational test evaluation, handover, organized crime, abuse of state
power, 7-layer authentication system, 2016 DIDC North Korean hacking, Root Server Concealment,
625 War Casualties Integrated Information System (Old KIATIS) Performance Improvement Project
(New KIATIS), Malicious Organized Nature, Individual Targeting, Evidence-Based Analysis,
Stakeholder Research, Digital Evidence, Accountability, Organized & Manipulated Evil

sections.title: 1. Introduction

sections.body:
1.1.1.

Research Background and Purpose
The 2016 North Korean Hacking Incident and the Failure of Institutional
Response

In September 2016, the Republic of Korea’s Defense Integrated Data Center (DIDC) 1st Center in
Yongin was penetrated by a North Korean cyber operation. In terms of scope and implications, this was
one of the most serious cybersecurity violations in the history of the Korean armed forces. A joint
investigation team, composed of the Military Prosecutor’s Office, the Defense Counterintelligence
Command, and the Cyber Operations Command, announced its conclusion in a formula that soon became
canonical: the incident had been caused by the simultaneous use of internet and defense network LAN
cards at DIDC’s 2nd Center in Gyeryongdae, malware had entered the system, and no classified
information had been compromised.
This official story was incomplete at best and deceptive at worst. It diverted attention from the DIDC
1st Center in Yongin and from the structural vulnerabilities of a particular system: the legacy 6·25 War
Dead Integrated Information System, commonly known as KIATIS. Instead of confronting the
uncomfortable fact that a critical defense database had been operated for years over the public internet
without basic security controls, the joint investigation redirected blame toward a convenient configuration
error in a different facility.
The present study demonstrates, on the basis of primary evidence, that the official explanation was
false. It shows that the true “root server” of the 2016 hacking incident was the legacy KIATIS hosted at
DIDC 1st Center, and that this system had been operated for roughly fifteen years in a manner that
violated both national information and communications security law and the Ministry of National
Defense (MND) Defense Informatization Directive.
The paper has a second, equally important purpose. It traces how, following the 2016 incident, a
constellation of organizations—including the Military Prosecutor’s Office, the MND’s Informatization
Planning Office, the National Defense Information Resources Management Institute (often referred to as
the National Defense Information Service), the DIDC, the Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA),
the Defense Counterintelligence Command, and the Cyber Operations Command—collectively engaged
in what I call an APT‑style human targeting operation against a single officer with thirty‑two years of
service. The operation culminated in his criminal labeling and dishonorable discharge.
Traditional cybersecurity research has focused on technical intrusions and system vulnerabilities. The
case analyzed here is of a different order. It concerns an organizational attack in which state institutions,
acting under the cover of legality, applied advanced persistent threat (APT) tactics not to computers but
to a person. The target was a career officer whose “offense” was to discover and articulate the structural
causes of the 2016 hacking incident.

sections.title: 1.1.2. The Concept and Characteristics of APT‑Style Human Targeting
sections.body:
In its original technical sense, an Advanced Persistent Threat combines three elements: a carefully
chosen target, a long‑term campaign, and sophisticated methods. In the cyber domain this means that an
attacker selects a specific organization or system, invests time in reconnaissance, maintains a presence
over months or years, and uses a range of tools—from spear‑phishing and zero‑day exploits to covert
channels—to achieve its objectives.
This paper extends the APT concept beyond networks and devices to what I term “APT‑style human
targeting”. In this extended sense, APT logic is applied simultaneously in digital, organizational, legal,
and socio‑psychological domains. The target is not an IP address, but a person embedded in an institution.
The “attack surface” consists of his professional role, his responsibilities, his reputation, his family life,
and his legal status.
The conception of APT‑style human targeting developed here rests on three pillars. The first is the
author’s more than thirty years of practical experience in information technology and cybersecurity
within the armed forces, including system development, acquisition, and cyber defense. The second is
a body of academic training in computer engineering, cybersecurity, and public policy, including
doctoral and master’s‑level research in empirical methods and policy analysis. The third, and most
decisive, is the author’s experience as the target of the very operation he seeks to describe. That
experience, while traumatic, opened a window onto internal mechanisms that would be difficult for
external observers to see.
From this vantage point, the core characteristics of APT‑style human targeting can be summarized as
follows. First, “targeting” refers to the prior, detailed analysis and profiling of a specific individual: his
expertise, personality, career trajectory, perceived vulnerabilities, and potential to disrupt established
interests. Second, “persistence” refers to the fact that the operation unfolds over an extended period—
months or years—through a sequence of administrative, legal, and social measures that maintain
continuous pressure and control. Third, “advancement” refers to the deployment of the organization’s
full arsenal of authority and resources—technical systems, legal instruments, surveillance capabilities,
audit powers, and prosecutorial discretion—in a coordinated and sophisticated manner.
These features parallel those of technical APT campaigns in structure and logic, even though the
medium is different. For this reason, the present case may represent one of the earliest fully documented
instances in which APT‑style thinking, originally developed for cyber operations, is applied to the
systematic targeting of an individual within a state apparatus. The implications extend beyond
cybersecurity to the study of organizational crime, human rights, and public law.

sections.title: 1.1.3. The Reality of Organized Conspiracy and Manipulation Against a
sections.body:
Thirty‑Two‑Year Career Officer
The seven‑layer proof system developed in this paper allows the reality of organized conspiracy and
manipulation against a single officer to be seen in sharp relief. A key moment occurred on 8 February
2022, during a meeting on “improvement directions” for the operation of the new KIATIS system. In
that meeting, a working‑level officer from the 6·25 Agency, speaking candidly, acknowledged that for
approximately fifteen years the legacy KIATIS had been operated over the internet, accessed remotely
without VPN, and used from devices such as Galaxy Note smartphones. This admission aligned with
the author’s own emerging understanding of the system’s history.
Forty‑eight hours later, on 10 February 2022, a junior officer filed an “abuse of power” complaint
against the author. Within days, the author was removed from his office and placed in a single‑person
room, where he spent roughly six months in isolation, cut off from normal information flows and social
contact. The official rationale was the need to protect subordinates from alleged abuse, but the timing
and context point strongly to another purpose: to neutralize a person who had begun to articulate the
structural link between the KIATIS configuration and the 2016 hacking incident.
On 25 April 2022, the Military Prosecutor’s Office opened an investigation. On 18 July 2022, three
years after the integrated development and operational test of the new KIATIS had been completed, the
author’s normal project management actions during that test were reclassified as criminal. The principal
charge was “obstruction of the performance of official duties by fraudulent means,” based on the
allegation that he had “manipulated the test environment” so that the system would appear to pass.
At the heart of the allegation was a firewall policy change at DIDC that allowed direct database access
from the DCIA test environment. The prosecution treated this change as an extraordinary, illicit attempt
to bypass security controls—specifically VPN and database access control systems. Yet a decisive
document, signed by one of the Cartel’s central figures, shows that from 27 August 2019 to 23 April
2021, VPN and database access control (the so‑called “SACRA MAX” system) were not in use for
KIATIS at all. In other words, the configuration the prosecution described as a fraudulent exception was,
in fact, the normal state of the system for at least a year and a half after the test.
On 11 October 2022, the Military Prosecutor’s Office issued a decision to suspend prosecution on six
charges, including obstruction of official duties, and on 31 October 2022 the author was forced to leave
the military under a dishonorable discharge. A thirty‑two‑year career ended not with recognition but
with stigma. His family life was devastated, and his reputation was gravely damaged.
The author’s response was not to withdraw but to document. Over four years he collected and analyzed
approximately 13,500 pages of evidence: official records, digital logs, audio recordings, witness
statements, interrogation transcripts, seizure warrants, and the non‑prosecution decision. From this
material he constructed the seven‑layer proof system that underpins the present paper, allowing the full
extent of the organizational crime to be made visible.
1.2.

Research Methodology

sections.title: 1.2.1. A Evidence‑Based “Method of Discovery”
sections.body:
APT‑style human targeting, by its nature, involves simultaneous pressure from multiple organizations
and channels over an extended period. It is not a single decision but an evolving pattern. To perceive
such a pattern, one must adopt an empirical, forensic stance: a “method of discovery”26 that lets the data
speak, even when those data are fragmentary or hostile.
This study grounds itself in the legal and regulatory framework that governs defense informatization
and national contracts. The primary legal sources include the Framework Act on Intelligent
Informatization, the National Contract Act, the Software Promotion Act, and other statutes regulating
public IT procurement and operation. At the level of secondary norms, the study examines the
Information System Audit Standards, the General Conditions for Service Contracts, the National
Information Security Basic Guidelines, and, centrally, the MND Defense Informatization Directive.
On that basis, the author carried out a phased analysis. First, he classified the key terms, concepts,
contract documents, and official letters that define and describe defense informatization projects,
including KIATIS. Second, he examined the meaning of these terms in their internal and external
contexts, tracing how their use changed across layers, organizations, and time. Third, he analyzed the
relationships between routine work actions and manipulative actions, distinguishing legitimate
implementation from covert tampering, and tracing causal chains from one to the other.
Statements by officials—whether in formal witness interviews, in interrogation transcripts, or in
recorded conversations—were not treated as raw truths but as discursive acts. By comparing what people
said in different settings and at different times, the author was able to identify inconsistencies, scripted
narratives, and points at which the official story diverged from documentary and technical reality. This
iterative, discovery‑oriented process allowed the seven layers of the scheme to emerge not as a
speculative hypothesis but as a grounded reconstruction.
The methodological stance was not chosen in comfort. It was, in the author’s words, “the only possible
choice, given where I stood.” During crucial periods he was confined to a single room, cut off from
informal networks and external researchers. As a result, he had to assume the dual role of victim and
investigator, collecting and interpreting evidence under conditions deliberately engineered to disable
him.

sections.title: 1.2.2. Construction and Application of the Seven‑Layer Proof System
sections.body:
26 Austin, J. L.'s work, Philosophical Papers (Oxford University Press, 1979), was referenced just as a tool for my
research methodology.
Beginning in May 2025, the author undertook a systematic re‑examination of the accumulated
evidence, with the explicit aim of reconstructing the Defense Informatization Cartel’s concealment and
manipulation across distinct phases. The result was the **seven‑layer proof system** that structures the
empirical part of this paper.
The “first layer” covers the period from 2017 to 2024 and analyzes how the Active‑X removal
initiative was used to erase the operational history of the legacy KIATIS, to conceal its long‑term
exposure to the internet, and to amend the Defense Informatization Directive in ways that obscured past
violations.
The “second layer” focuses on the years 2018 to 2022 and examines the manipulation of the new
KIATIS project’s promotion structure, project approvals, project plans, and Requests for Proposals. It
shows how these documents were crafted to redistribute and blur responsibility and to prepare future
blame‑shifting.
The “third layer” covers the period from 3 December 2018 to 18 October 2019, when the author served
as software development project manager at DCIA. It establishes that his work in that role was
substantively normal and compliant with applicable rules, and, crucially, that he did not participate in,
and was not in a position to control, the integrated development and operational test that later became
the focus of criminal accusations.
The “fourth layer” examines the period from 3 December 2018 to 11 September 2019, focusing on
the orchestration of the new KIATIS development and operational test. It documents the construction
of an artificial test environment, the exclusion of the actual operational environment from evaluation,
the manipulation of KIDA’s evaluation research results, and the use of these results to justify
amendments to the Defense Informatization Directive.
The “fifth layer” analyzes the events from late February to September 2022, beginning with the 8
February meeting and the subsequent false abuse complaint. It reconstructs the author’s six‑month
solitary confinement, the orchestrated reputational attacks, and the use of these measures to break his
resistance and discredit his claims.
The “sixth layer” covers the Military Prosecutor’s targeted investigation from April to October 2022.
It shows how normal project management actions in 2019 were retroactively reinterpreted as crimes,
how seizure warrants and investigation notices were drafted to fit a pre‑determined narrative, and how
the final non‑prosecution decision was used to impose a criminal label while avoiding judicial scrutiny.
The “seventh layer” deals with the period from October 2022 to the present. It tracks the author’s
petitions to internal and external bodies, the responses (or lack thereof) from the Minister of National
Defense, the Prosecutor’s Office, the Anti‑Corruption and Civil Rights Commission, and the National
Human Rights Commission, and the way in which these bodies, by dismissing or deflecting his claims,
completed the Cartel’s effort to normalize and close the case.

> 📊 **Figure 2 — Visualization of the integrated 7-layer proof system of this thesis**

![Figure_2_7Layer_Proof_System_Visualization.png](./images/Figure_2_7Layer_Proof_System_Visualization.png)

Each layer forms an independent evidentiary module, yet the modules are tightly interconnected.
Together they reveal a single, continuous system of organizational crime extending over eight years,
from 2017 to 2025. The corpus of primary material comprises approximately 13,500 pages of documents:
evidence records, audio transcript lists, witness statements, interrogation transcripts, seizure warrants,
non‑prosecution decisions, and related materials. Wherever relevant, the analysis also draws on U.S.
Department of Defense standards for Developmental Test and Evaluation (DT&E) and Operational Test
and Evaluation (OT&E), in order to contextualize and critique the technical and procedural deviations
observed in the KIATIS case. <Figure 2> visualizes the 7-Layer proof system of this thesis.

sections.title: 1.2.3. Analysis of National Contract Law, Defense Regulations, and Official
sections.body:
Documents
The research proceeds under a guiding conviction: “Later, it will become clear that there was no other
way for me.” To test and verify this conviction, the author subjected three main classes of sources to
systematic analysis: the National Contract Act and related laws, the MND’s Defense Informatization
Directive and associated instructions, and the universe of official documents stored in the On‑nara
system, together with related statements.
The “National Contract Act” governs contracts in which the state is a party. In the new KIATIS project,
the Act required that the scope of work be defined in the Request for Proposals, that contracts be
executed within that scope, and that evaluation committees not unilaterally impose major new
obligations without corresponding contractual adjustments. The author shows that the evaluation
committee’s decision to adopt eighty additional requirements, not contained in the original Request for
Proposals, amounted to a clear violation of the Act and of the principles of fair and transparent
procurement.
The “Defense Informatization Directive” is the central regulatory instrument governing defense IT
projects, including planning, management, testing, and acceptance. By tracing the Directive’s
amendments from 2015 to 2023, the author shows how key provisions were altered in ways that
facilitated the concealment of legacy KIATIS and the manipulation of new projects. A particularly
striking example is the change, in the 4 June 2020 version (No. 2436), of the principle that “development
testing and operational testing shall be separated” into a formulation that effectively made integrated
testing the default. The paper argues, using elementary tools from logic and systems engineering, that
this change is not merely a policy adjustment but a normative inversion akin to asking a system to satisfy
contradictory constraints.
The “On‑nara official document system” records the creation, approval, transmission, and reception
of all formal communications among government agencies. By examining On‑nara records, the author
was able to reconstruct who wrote which document, when it was drafted and approved, who received it,
and how its content related (or failed to relate) to the actual state of systems and projects at the time. In
several instances, the timestamps and contents of documents cannot be reconciled without assuming
backdating or post hoc editing; in other instances, missing or altered phrases point to stealthy regulatory
“laundering.”
Finally, the analysis of “statements by relevant actors”—official witness statements, interrogation
transcripts, and recordings of conversations—provides a human dimension to the documentary record.
These sources reveal patterns of coordinated messaging, rhetorical strategies for minimizing
responsibility, and moments when interviewees inadvertently confirm facts that written documents try
to obscure.

sections.title: 1.2.4. Comparative Analysis with Hannah Arendt's 'Banality of Evil'
sections.body:
The 2022 military prosecution's targeted investigation had the following background. The internet
server hosting the old KIATIS operations was the root server, and this was the cause of the 2016 North
Korean hacking incident. To cover this up, they carried out a long-term, organized, collective, and highly
planned operation. As a byproduct, systematic collusion, fabrication, concealment, and unconscionable
human rights violations were exposed. This differs from the "banality of evil" observed at World War
II crime scenes. It represents a sophisticated, precision-engineered case where an organization of digitalage information specialists mobilized acquired cybersecurity techniques to hunt a single individual in a
many-against-one structure until their desired objective was achieved.
The targeted individual can be destroyed in fear and humiliation, and in extreme cases, their life is
threatened. This paper was written after three years of waiting and suffering following a shipwreck
against such evil. Evidence is presented in the order of a seven-layered proof system, based on analysis
of the National Contract Act, Ministry of National Defense directives and regulations, project documents,
On-Nara official documents, and statements from related parties.
1.3.

Significance and Limitations of the Study

sections.title: 1.3.1. Academic Significance
sections.body:
The study makes substantive contributions to several fields. In “cybersecurity studies”, it extends the
APT concept from purely technical operations to organizational and legal campaigns targeting
individuals, thereby opening a new line of inquiry into “human APTs” within institutions. In
“organizational crime research”, it provides a detailed case study of how role segmentation,
responsibility diffusion, and moral compartmentalization can be combined with high technical expertise
to produce a sophisticated form of organizational wrongdoing. In “legal studies”, it demonstrates the
feasibility and necessity of digital‑evidence‑based accountability, showing how logs, directives, and
official documents can be used to reconstruct chains of responsibility in complex public projects. In
“human rights scholarship”, it offers a granular account of how state power, exercised through
apparently lawful procedures, can destroy an individual’s life and dignity.
Methodologically, the study is also an unusual example of **insider research**. It is based not on
second‑hand access to files but on the direct, painstaking collection and analysis of primary materials
by the person who was both subject and observer of the events. This dual position entails obvious risks
of bias, but it also enables a degree of depth and contextual understanding that external researchers rarely
achieve, especially in opaque security institutions.

sections.title: 1.3.2. Social Significance
sections.body:
Socially, the case serves as a contemporary illustration of how national security rhetoric can be used
to justify, and conceal, the abuse of state power. It shows how prosecutorial offices, audit bodies, and
research institutes can drift from their mandates and become instruments of concealment rather than of
truth‑seeking.
At the same time, the study highlights the potential of **digital traces** to counter such abuses. In a
highly informatized state, virtually every decision leaves a footprint—in logs, in version histories, in
email headers, in document management systems. The author’s reconstruction of the KIATIS affair
demonstrates that, even when institutions attempt to manipulate or destroy evidence, enough residual
traces may remain to make responsibility traceable for those willing to look closely.

sections.title: 1.3.3. Limitations
sections.body:
The study has several limitations. First, it is conducted by the principal victim of the events it describes,
and is therefore unavoidably exposed to the risk of subjective bias. The author has attempted to mitigate
this risk by grounding every major claim in documentary evidence, by cross‑checking statements across
independent sources, and by adopting a structured proof format that makes inferences explicit.
Nonetheless, readers should bear in mind the positionality from which the analysis is written.
Second, the study has the character of a detailed case study. Its findings cannot be mechanically
generalized to all defense organizations or all cases of alleged misconduct. However, the conceptual
tools developed here—the seven‑layer proof system and the notion of APT‑style human targeting—are,
in principle, applicable to other cases where complex organizational wrongdoing is suspected.
Third, the study necessarily relies on the evidence that could be obtained by a single individual under
conditions of constraint. Some classes of information—such as informal instructions delivered orally,
non‑recorded meetings, and fully deleted electronic files—remain inaccessible. It is therefore possible
that certain aspects of the scheme remain unknown. Even so, the body of evidence presented is more
than sufficient to establish the existence of a coherent, multi‑layered organizational crime.

sections.title: 1.3.4. Directions for Future Research
sections.body:
The analysis opens several avenues for further work. There is a need to refine the theory of “APT‑style
human targeting”, distinguishing it more sharply from related concepts such as workplace bullying,
strategic lawsuits against public participation, and classic persecution of whistleblowers. Comparative
studies of similar cases, both within and beyond the defense sector, would help to clarify which aspects
of this case are specific and which are symptomatic of broader patterns.
Further research is also needed on “digital‑evidence‑based accountability mechanisms”. This includes
technical work on secure logging and tamper‑evident records, legal work on evidentiary standards for
digital traces, and organizational work on how to protect and empower those who seek to use such
evidence to challenge powerful institutions.
Finally, the study points to the necessity of exploring “institutional reforms” to prevent the recurrence
of such cases: reforms in the design of oversight bodies, in the protection of whistleblowers, in the
governance of defense informatization projects, and in the training and ethical formation of prosecutors,
auditors, and IT professionals within the state.
The present paper does not claim to solve these problems. It aims instead to provide a detailed,
evidence‑based account of one case in which they have already materialized. In doing so, it seeks to
contribute both to scholarly understanding and to the broader public conversation about how democratic
societies can and should respond when their own institutions become the source of an advanced,
persistent threat to the very people who serve them.

> 📊 **Figure 3 — Cyberspace centered on the Republic of Korea's defense**

![Figure_3_ROK_Defense_Cyberspace.png](./images/Figure_3_ROK_Defense_Cyberspace.png)

sections.title: 2. Theoretical Background and Conceptual Definitions
2.1. Literature Review
sections.body:
As the world has been confronted with the unexpected and inescapable phenomenon of cyber threat,
we need an innovative way of responding to the cyber threats in cyberspace.
Regarding cyberspace security, many studies conducted before and around the mid-2010s have
strongly indicated that it has its own characteristics, such as a complex, uncertain, ambiguous and timecritical social threatening issues in cyberspace (Azuma et al., 2006; Eom et al., 2012; Guitouni et al.,
2006; Soliman & Janz, 2004; Tsakanyan, 2017; Hukill, 2012, Karnouskos, 2011) argued that the speed
to influence the effect demands lightning-quick reactions in a timely manner in cyberspace.
Considering this treatise is a trying to get the insights by searching for the scene of long-term APTstyle human targeting, which is beyond cybersecurity, it is crucial to understand the scope of the
cyberspace. Its scope can be across the layer of cyberspace from a nation consisting of civilian, public,
and military areas to those worldwide. As shown in <Figure 3>, cyberspace, led by the internet, which
has become part of daily life, differs from the traditional military domain: the actors differ by domain
(civilian, public, and defense), and their missions and roles differ. The defense domain’s internet
network, Defense Network, and battlefield network are parts of the overall cyberspace, and generally,
information protection systems (cyber assets) provide prevention, detection, response, and recovery
from cyber threats. These three separate systems that make up the defense domain exchange data through
interconnections (data exchange systems, etc.) or storage media (Youngju LEE, 2016).
