Similarly, Friedman et al. (2012) posit that the scope of damage in and through cyberspace is getting
larger in proportion with the level of the dependence on one nation´s digitalization by its global nature
of cyberspace. The feature of cyberspace further substantiates this argument—an ill-structured problem
in which the extreme complexity to identify the intent and motivation of the attacking party exists
basically due to the openness of cyberspace.
Likewise, Gore et al. (2006) argued that from the dynamic and uncertain features in cyberspace, it is
hard for a decision-maker to find the appropriate course of action and response timely. Those
characteristics provide us with an insight into why it is necessary for cyberspace security to need a
different dimensional approach compared with the traditional decision-making approach. The traditional
one usually has so called hierarchical unitary allocation of decision-making rights—hierarchical unitary
decision-making rights, tightly controlled cooperation interaction and restricted information sharing
(Alberts et al., 2001; Stone, 2016).
Before proceeding further, it is necessary to define clearly the key terminology. The term, the
decision-making process, which is called the command and control in the military (Pigeau & Carol,
2001, see also Hukill, 2012; Lawson, 1981) is taken to mean as the comprehensive terminology that can
be applied to individual, private, public and international in order to conduct cyberspace security
effectively across all the sectors. In this wide-ranging terminology, we can effectively express various
tasks of cyberspace security across all the sectors independently with its own counter-measure systems
while remaining able to conduct collaborated ones interdependently in an interoperable and need-toknow basis.
Also, we need to define what cyberspace security is. Cyberspace security here includes all the
activities related to guarantee cyberspace to be stable, secure and peaceful. And it also includes the
active response as well as collecting the threats information executed for destruction or damage. From
that definition, we can draw that cyberspace security covers all activities to prevent, mitigate, respond
to, protect and recover from cyber threats in the all the sectors including private, public, military areas
and beyond the national side. So, cyberspace security can be used to indicate a condition of a secure and
reliable cyberspace, further to the national and international security in a broad sense of manner (Alberts
et al., 2001; Stone, 2016). On the other hand, some authors asserted that the new decision-making
process should also reflect a comprehensive readiness posture based on the roles and responsibilitybased collaborations while activating the agile and timely action for deciding and acting rapidly more
than adversaries. (see Alberts et al., 2001; cf. Brehmer, 2005; Gore et al., 2009; Hurwitz, 2014; Klein,
2008; Kramer et al., 2009; Simon, 1973).
Not only the unique characteristics—uncertain, ambiguous and time critical, but also the nature of
cyberspace security, win or lose results by the power of technique and knowledge (e.g., Clarke & Knake,
2014; Friedman & Singer, 2016) are urgently demanding to create the new adaptive decision-making
process based on the roles and responsibility-based collaborations. Furthermore, considering those
continuously changing situations, we can infer that it is also required to apply data-centric situational
awareness or seen the process of conceptualizing and designing the APT-Style Individual Targeted
Attacks. In this regard, Hoadley and Lucas (2018) have concluded that the demanding of agility and the
exclusive characteristics of cyberspace can explain why we are needed to consider the cyberspace
security the whole way based on the roles and responsibilities and collaborations of all the parties.
In fact, when we need to seek out the similarities of the characteristics of cyberspace security from
the physical, cyberspace security needs to identify similar successful factors as we mention in the next
section, 2.2. Moreover, it can also be identified through the design and execution of the APT-Style
Individual Targeted Attacks in various other challenging issues (see Gergen, 1973). Furthermore, the
efforts to identify similar patterns would be helpful to figure out the suitability of applying cyberspace
security in the same lens of other challenging social issues, which have before applied to the structures
of the APT-Style Individual Targeted Attacks.
It is also argued that cyberspace security must be considered in a full spectrum way (Hill, 2015;
Tsakanyan, 2017). It is also obligatory for cyberspace to tackle socially cross-sectorial challenging
issues while reflecting the features of the APT-Style Individual Targeted Attacks. This adaptable
approach can give us the insight to figure out the commonality and differences from previous studies.
Many studies have attempted to argue that it is crucial to embrace the power of advanced technology
such as artificial intelligence, data-centric economy, and business (Bernier, 2010; Clarke et al., 2014;
Hill,2015; Hoadley, 2018; Kuehl, 2009; Singer & Friedman, 2014; Stevens, 2013; Tsakanyan, 2017;
Wielki, 2006; Youngju, 2016). Due to the anticipation of widespread influence of the APT-Style
Individual Targeted Attacks beyond the boundaries of nations and across the political, economic, social,
military, and others, it is crucial to embrace and foster the required technologies and attendant issues
arising from the 4IR transformation (Shava & Hofisi, 2017).
According to the recent studies (viz., Eom et al., 2012; Lee, 2016), not only due to our daily life is
interwoven with cyberspace but also various actors with their different purpose and means in cyberspace,
capabilities coming from artificial intelligence and its related advance technologies should be implanted
on cyberspace security issues. Therefore, as we establish a concept and implementation of a missionoriented approach to cyber security, AI related science and technology and advanced mechanisms to
identify, detect, and respond in a timely manner should be considered to protect the negative influence
across all social places—public and private sectors.

sections.title: 2.2. Cyberspace as a Mirror Image of Behavior Patterns of a Human
sections.body:
Being

Here, we briefly examine the relationship between behavioral patterns in physical space and
cyberspace to enhance understanding of the key issues arising across these two domains in this treatise.
As seen in Figure 2-2, cyberspace can be conceptualized as two facing spaces: the real world and the
virtual world. These two worlds can be represented as interconnected components composed of human
elements—such as software, hardware, and networks—existing within the real or physical world.
Human will, which creates and destroys diverse virtual relationships, will be dynamically expressed in

> 📊 **Figure 4 — Cyberspace as a Mirror Image of Behavior Patterns of a Human Being**

![Figure_4_Cyberspace_Mirror_Image_Behavior_Patterns.png](./images/Figure_4_Cyberspace_Mirror_Image_Behavior_Patterns.png)

this combined world. Cyberspace enables us humans to live in another new world called the virtual
world.
![Figure 4](./images/Figure_4_Cyberspace_Mirror_Image_Behavior_Patterns.png)
**<Figure 4> Cyberspace as a Mirror Image of Behavior Patterns of a Human Being**

As cyberspace becomes increasingly intertwined, we realize that the virtual world is rapidly
approaching the point where it will break down the boundaries between the two paired realms, driven
by the exponential advancement of technological innovations such as artificial intelligence and big data.
Consequently, as the virtual world substantially expands humanity's existential living sphere, cyberspace
will increasingly resemble a mirror image of the real world. Consequently, cyberspace enables humans
to inhabit another new world: the virtual world. Cyberspace thus enters the fabric of daily life. Most
nations have cultivated cyberspace as a core domain of national security.
The real and virtual worlds share a common characteristic—namely, that they are created and
developed by human will in response to needs and desires (Gergen, 1973). For this reason, similar social
behavior patterns found in sociology and social psychology can also be observed within the cyber space
of the virtual world. For instance, good deeds in the real world are analogous to knowledge sharing on
social media, while hostility towards cyber-attacks or threats, followers' sympathy and empathy towards
attacks by domestic hackers, and obedience and compliance are other examples (Clarke & Knake, 2014;
Hurwitz, 2014; Kuehl, 2009).
The examples cited here represent merely tentative attempts at simple mapping to discern behavioral
pattern relationships between the two domains. Actual behavioral patterns can spread extensively or
converge multifacetedly across diverse domains—political, economic, social, military—depending on
the perpetrator's objectives and opportunities for achieving satisfaction. Such accomplishments are
generally realized through cyberspace, functioning as an indirect mediating device transcending national
boundaries (Kuehl, 2009; Friedman & Singer, 2016).
Because of the inherent nature of being man-made, situations in the communities of the real world
also frequently take place in cyberspace, making it difficult for us to anticipate the course of actions or
events. Moreover, due to the advent of technological singularity of the AI as well as the effect of the
counteraction rooted from the characteristics of cyberspace and malicious will of human beings,
cyberspace has been a core place to pursue national interests by militarizing it and by obtaining preemptive, incomparable power through cyberspace (Kramer et al., 2009; Stevens, 2013).
As cyber threats emerge in both the real and virtual worlds, this complexity will increase
exponentially. The causes of this complexity lie in physical locations and virtual spaces, people and
technology, and human will. Detecting, identifying, diagnosing, responding to, and devising appropriate
countermeasures for dynamic and uncertain attack and threat scenarios exceeds human capability. These
attempts, which bring about exponentially unexpected consequences, will serve as a good guide to
establishing an understanding of why we must acquire, cultivate, and utilize the capabilities of artificial
intelligence.
Above all, threats persistently exploit diverse vulnerabilities based on the actual patterns of human
behaviors occurring within these two domains, with the resulting damage escalating from individuals to
nations and even between nations. Among these, the most threatening hacking attack known to date
within cyberspace is the APT attack.
2.3.

APT and APT‑Style Human Targeting

sections.title: 2.3.1. The APT Concept in Cybersecurity
sections.body:
In contemporary cybersecurity, the “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)” model has become a central
framework for understanding highly sophisticated and dangerous attacks. APT campaigns are generally
defined by three attributes: they are “advanced”, in the sense that they employ tailored tools,
vulnerabilities, and tactics; “persistent”, in that they are maintained over long periods; and “threatening”,
in that they are directed at high‑value targets such as state institutions, large corporations, and critical
infrastructure.
Unlike opportunistic hacking, which often aims at quick intrusion and immediate gain, APT
campaigns are built around long‑term infiltration and sustained data exfiltration. They typically unfold
over months or years and involve careful reconnaissance, staged compromises, lateral movement within
networks, and ongoing efforts to evade detection. The core structural features of APTs can be
summarized as follows.
First, APTs are “target‑specific”. Attackers select a particular organization or individual and study
them in depth before designing customized attack vectors. Second, they are “persistent”. Rather than a
single intrusion, the attack consists of a series of probes, footholds, and reinforcements that maintain
access over time. Third, they are “stealthy”. Attack activities are disguised as normal traffic or routine
operations, masking malicious behavior within legitimate patterns. Fourth, they are “multi‑stage”. From
initial compromise to final objective, attackers employ a sequence of tactics—exploiting client‑side
vulnerabilities, moving laterally between systems, escalating privileges, and exfiltrating or corrupting
data.
Seen through this lens, the vulnerabilities exposed in the KIATIS case become clearer. The legacy
KIATIS system possessed structural weaknesses that made it almost ideal as a foothold for APT‑style
operations. As an Active‑X‑based web application, it exposed client‑side vulnerabilities. The absence
of VPNs and database access control mechanisms meant that perimeter defenses were largely nominal.
More seriously, KIATIS was architected in such a way that core defense functions were performed over
the public internet rather than within a segregated defense network.
This design effectively collapsed the boundary between the external internet and the internal defense
network, providing attackers with a “persistent access environment”. Remote access to the database
from mobile devices such as Galaxy Note smartphones exemplified the vulnerabilities associated with
a poorly managed “bring your own device” environment. At the same time, the presence of
data‑exchange mechanisms linking the internet and defense networks created ideal paths for lateral
movement. For more than a decade, information flows between internet and defense domains remained
open and weakly controlled, aligning precisely with the persistence and lateral mobility that make APT
campaigns so effective.
These weaknesses were not merely technical flaws; they constituted serious violations of higher‑level
security instruments, including the Defense Cybersecurity Directive and national information security
laws. Nevertheless, as later layers of this study show, the test and evaluation processes were manipulated
so that these realities were distorted or concealed. Attackers—whether external or internal—could
leverage the data‑exchange channels to establish a foothold in the internal environment and to move
through it under cover of normal traffic.
The Military Prosecutor’s Office eventually charged the author with, among other things, violating
DIDC security regulations by failing to use VPNs and database access controls in the test environment
and by requesting firewall port openings that allegedly created a test environment “different from the
real operational environment” without informing the evaluation committee. On the surface this
accusation appears technical and precise. In reality, it collapses under a single document: an internal
letter that confirms that, in the new KIATIS environment, VPNs and database access controls were not
in use between 27 August 2019 and at least 23 April 2021. The objects that prosecutors claimed the
author had “switched off” as part of a scheme to manipulate the test did not, at that time, exist as
operational controls; they were still absent eighteen months later. Ironically, the document that proves
this fact bears the signature of a central figure in the Defense Informatization Cartel.
What occurred here is a classic instance of “victim blaming”. Responsibility for fifteen years of
neglected structural vulnerabilities was shifted onto an individual who had not designed, approved, or
operated the system in its original form. A system architected from the outset without appropriate
security devices was reimagined as a system whose security had been intentionally disabled by a single
project manager. The narrative thus crafted mirrors the social‑engineering aspects of APT operations: a
carefully constructed story that induces others to assign blame to the wrong entity.
In conclusion, the legacy KIATIS system was structurally defenseless against APT‑style attacks, and
this vulnerability was left unaddressed for more than a decade. The decision to recast this systemic
failure as the fault of a single officer, and to criminalize him under the charge of “obstruction of the
performance of official duties,” is itself an example of an APT‑style organizational attack that turns a
victim into a perpetrator.

sections.title: 2.3.2. Extension from Systems to Individuals Inside Organizations
sections.body:
The logic of targeting in APT campaigns can be extended from systems to individuals. In
organizational settings, personal targeting proceeds through a combination of formal authority and
informal influence. Actors who possess institutional power—superiors, staff officers, auditors,
prosecutors, external researchers, and maintenance contractors—are mobilized to align their actions
with the objectives of the Cartel. Those who are weaker or more dependent within this network become
“weak links” through which pressure can be applied.
In the legal and investigative domain, warrants, statements, and procedural documents provide the
framework for a “targeted investigation narrative”. In the social and psychological domain,
stigmatization, rumors about mental health, and engineered isolation destroy the target’s defenses and
support networks. These different dimensions are not accidental by‑products; they are coordinated
elements of a strategy designed to make self‑defense impossible.
Such a strategy leaves identifiable traces. First, there is “simultaneous pressure from multiple agencies
and channels” directed at the same person. Second, there are “sudden and poorly justified changes in
personnel status and reputation”, including abrupt isolation, reassignment, or demotion. Third, there are
“anomalies in the timing and content of document revisions”, including repeated changes in approval
chains and edits that are difficult to reconcile with the physical timeline. Fourth, there are “gaps or
inconsistencies in system logs and metadata”, suggesting tampering or selective preservation.
All of these indicators appear in the present case. The February 2022, meeting, in which the legacy
KIATIS’s long‑term operation without VPN was openly mentioned, was followed exactly forty‑eight
hours later by a false abuse complaint. This is consistent with the pattern of multi‑agency, rapid response
against an emerging internal threat. Numerous examples of temporal mismatch in official documents—
where drafting dates, approval dates, and content do not cohere—point to post hoc editing and
backdating. Such patterns constitute documentary evidence that the operational reality was rewritten at
the record level.

sections.title: 2.3.3. APT‑Style Targeting When the Medium Is Human, Not Machine
sections.body:
APT‑style human targeting27 represents the “projection of APT logic from digital systems into social,
legal, and organizational space”. The target is no longer only a server or network; it is a person whose
roles, relationships, and vulnerabilities can be mapped and exploited.
The operation begins with “profiling”. The target’s duties, network of relationships, technical
expertise, and personal traits are analyzed. In the KIATIS case, this involved, among other things,
recognizing that the author’s integrity, professional experience, and unwillingness to compromise on
security issues made him simultaneously valuable and dangerous to the Cartel.
The next phase is “infiltration”, which in human terms means manipulating access to information,
controlling what the target sees and does not see, and introducing distortions into the target’s
environment. In the technical domain this took the form of restricting system access for the author while
preserving and enhancing access for others. In the organizational domain it involved reassigning tasks
and responsibilities, altering approval chains, and subtly recalibrating roles so that the target would be
left exposed but marginalized.
Subsequently, the operation proceeds through “lateral movement”. Just as technical APTs move
between systems, human APTs move between departments and organizations. In the KIATIS affair, this
meant coordinating actions among DCIA, the DIDC, the Agency, KIDA, audit bodies,
counterintelligence, and the Military Prosecutor’s Office. Each of these entities carried out actions that,
in isolation, could be portrayed as routine; together they advanced the campaign against the target.

27 I wish to clarify upfront that the insights presented regarding APT-style individual targeting are based on my 30
years of experience in the IT and cyber operations fields, my research experience during my master's and doctoral
studies, and, most importantly, my direct experience of being attacked—an experience that appears to be the first
documented case.
The final stages are “data collection, narrative construction, and objective realization”. Evidence is
selectively collected and shaped into a story that depicts the target as the source of problems. In the
KIATIS case, normal project management acts were retroactively framed as manipulations of the test
environment, and the target’s professional insistence on security concerns was recast as unreasonable or
abusive behavior. The ultimate objective was achieved when the target was branded a criminal,
discharged dishonorably, and stripped of his credibility as a witness.
From initial profiling to final criminal labeling, the sequence mirrors the multi‑stage structure 28 of
technical APT campaigns. In both domains, the attacker invests in understanding the target, maintains
pressure across multiple vectors, and adjusts tactics in response to defenses. The primary difference is that
in APT‑style human targeting, the payload is not malware but a “story”—a narrative that redefines a
defender of security as a source of insecurity.

sections.title: 2.4. Mechanisms
sections.body:
of
Organized
Conspiracy,
Manipulation,
Concealment, and Heinous Human Rights Violations

sections.title: 2.4.1. Role Differentiation and Diffusion of Responsibility
sections.body:
In cyber‑mediated white‑collar crime, strategic and tactical actions in cyberspace or through cyberspace
often serve three objectives. The first is to “conceal damage inflicted on the state”. The second is to
“preserve individual interests and protect the organization”. The third is to “disperse responsibility among
all stakeholders involved in the original failure”, in this case the 2016 hacking incident.
The core mechanism that supports all three objectives is “role differentiation combined with diffusion
of responsibility”. This mechanism ensures that no single actor experiences the full moral weight of the
crime. As Hannah Arendt observed in her discussion of the “banality of evil,” individual participants can
tell themselves that they are merely performing limited roles within a larger system, thereby disowning
responsibility for the consequences of their collective actions.
In the present case, the Military Prosecutor’s Office, the MND’s informatization units, DCIA, DIDC,
the Military Counterintelligence Command (MSSC), the Criminal Investigation Command (CIC), and
KIDA all contributed within their respective domains. KIDA, under the guise of “research,” provided an
analytical justification for the manipulated test environment. The Informatization Planning Office, using
the formal authority of “regulation amendment,” created a legal basis for the concealment of violations.
DIDC, as an infrastructure operator, configured server environments in ways that obscured the continuity
between legacy and new systems. The Defense Audit bodies and the Ministry’s Legal Affairs provided the
appearance of “inspection” and “discipline” by staging sham investigations. The Military Prosecutor’s
Office used its “investigative” powers to fix the blame on a single individual.
Each actor could plausibly claim to be operating within its professional remit. Each could say, “We were
just doing our job.” The structure thereby created makes accountability extremely difficult. No single

28 This remains an area requiring further empirical research.
organization appears to own the entire crime; each one can point to others. Yet from a systemic perspective,
these apparently routine acts combine to form a coherent, multi‑year scheme.
This pattern aligns with what organizational psychology and sociology describe as the “diffusion of
responsibility” phenomenon: when responsibility is spread across many hands, the sense of individual
accountability is attenuated. People find it easier to participate in harmful actions when they perceive
themselves as small parts of a larger machine rather than as moral agents.29

sections.title: 2.4.2. Blocking the Emergence of Guilt
sections.body:
For organized crime to persist within institutions, it is not enough to divide roles; the “emergence of
guilt” must also be suppressed. Social psychology provides a useful lens here.
Cognitive dissonance theory30 holds that individuals experience psychological discomfort when their
actions contradict their moral beliefs. One way to resolve this discomfort is to “distort one’s perception of
reality” so that actions and beliefs appear to align. In institutional settings, several mechanisms facilitate
this distortion.
The first is “fragmentation and specialization of work”, which restricts each actor’s awareness of the
broader context. Information is compartmentalized, and employees see only a truncated slice of the overall
process. As a result, they cannot easily perceive how their contributions fit into a larger harmful pattern.
The second is “culture of obedience to authority”. In hierarchical organizations such as the military,
obedience is not merely an operational requirement; it is a moral virtue. When individuals can say, “I was
following orders,” they shift perceived responsibility upward, to superiors and to “the system.” This is
reflected in records where officials justify their actions by reference to directives or to instructions from
higher headquarters.
The third mechanism is “groupthink” and the suppression of dissent. When an organization converges
on a single dominant narrative—such as “the system is secure,” or “the problem lies with a particular
individual”—internal criticism becomes risky. Those who raise questions are marginalized or punished. In
such environments, it becomes easier for the majority to convince themselves that they are defending,
rather than betraying, their institution.
A particularly potent variant of these mechanisms in the KIATIS case was the construction of a narrative
that transformed the victim into a perpetrator. By defining a long‑serving, conscientious officer as a
“criminal,” organizational members could reinterpret their actions as “enforcing justice.” This is precisely
the process described in Albert Bandura’s theory of “moral disengagement”, in which harmful behavior is
reframed as serving socially valuable goals, thereby suspending the actor’s usual moral standards.

29 The most striking example of this division of roles, as illustrated in this paper, is a 30-minute phone conversation
between the author and an official from the Anti-Corruption and Civil Rights Commission. That official made
every conceivable attempt to legally provide the thousands of pages of Ministry of National Defense corruption
information I had supplied back to the Ministry itself. It defies ordinary imagination.
30 Leon Festinger, translated by Kim Chang-dae, Cognitive Dissonance Theory , Seoul: Nanam Publishing, 2016.
In the KIATIS affair, prosecutors and other participants could tell themselves that they were defending
the integrity of defense projects, protecting the public purse, or safeguarding the reputation of national
security institutions, even as they helped to destroy the credibility and livelihood of a whistleblower. The
more fully they embraced this narrative, the less room remained for guilt.

sections.title: 2.4.3. Power Maintenance and Self‑Interest as Driving Motivations
sections.body:
The motivation behind the Cartel’s actions can be understood at both organizational and individual levels.
At the organizational level, the dominant motive was “power maintenance” and the protection of
“institutional legitimacy”. If the long‑standing structural vulnerabilities of KIATIS, and their role in the
2016 hacking incident, were openly acknowledged, the credibility of the Ministry’s informatization
policies and the reputation of key agencies would be severely damaged. 31 Questions would arise not
only about technical competence but also about prior oversight and honesty in public communications. In
such a scenario, the safest course for those in power was to redefine a systemic failure as an isolated,
personal failure.
At the individual level, actors were motivated by “career preservation” and “avoidance of legal liability”.
Senior officials who had approved or overseen the original KIATIS configuration had obvious reasons to
fear exposure. Even those who had not made the initial decisions might suffer reputational harm if their
organizations were implicated. By redirecting blame toward a single project manager and framing the issue
as a question of his alleged misconduct, they could protect their careers and avoid scrutiny.32
A crucial instrument in this process was “control over information”. By isolating the author in a single
room, cutting him off from colleagues, and managing what documents he could see and when, the Cartel
ensured that he could neither mobilize internal support nor gather evidence quickly enough to challenge
the narratives being constructed around him. This mirrors the logic of George Orwell’s depiction of
information control in “1984”33: those who control information control reality itself.
The events following the February 2022, meeting illustrate this dynamic. Once the legacy KIATIS’s
security weaknesses were spoken aloud, the Cartel moved rapidly to contain the threat. Within forty‑eight
hours, a false abuse complaint initiated the author’s isolation. Thereafter, internal communications about
KIATIS proceeded in channels from which he was excluded, while his own communications were subject
to scrutiny and reinterpretation. In this way, the Cartel preserved its informational advantage and thereby
its power.
Taken together, these mechanisms—role differentiation and diffusion of responsibility, the blocking of
guilt through cognitive and moral disengagement, and power and self‑interest as driving motives—form a

31 The root server from which the 2016 North Korean hacking originated was DIDC1 Center (Yongin), not DIDC2
Center (Gyeryongdae).
32 If structural security vulnerabilities neglected for 15 years are exposed, fundamental questions about the Ministry
of National Defense's overall information policy will inevitably arise.
33 Hannah Arendt, trans. Lee Jin-woo, The Human Condition, Hangilsa. 2019. Gergen, K. J. (1973). Social psychology
as history. Journal of personality and social psychology, 26(2),
coherent structure. Over eight years they enabled what this paper has called an APT‑style organizational
crime: a sustained, multi‑layered operation in which technical, legal, and social instruments were fused to
conceal systemic failures and to sacrifice a single human being in their place.

sections.title: 3. Analysis of the 7-Layer Proof System
3.1. Layer 1: Removal of Legacy KIATIS History During the Active‑X
sections.body:
Elimination Project

Layer 1 establishes the foundational factual claim of the seven‑layer proof system: that the legacy
KIATIS system was operated for approximately fifteen years, from 2007 to 2022, directly over the public
internet without VPN or database access control, and that the Defense Informatization Cartel used the
pretext of an “Active‑X elimination” project to erase or neutralize this history. This layer explains both
“why” the Cartel needed to engage in long‑term organized concealment and “how” it began to do so.

sections.title: 3.1.1. Operation of Legacy KIATIS on the Internet: Findings and Proofs
3.1.1.1. Finding 1: Legacy KIATIS Operated for Fifteen Years on Non‑Dedicated
sections.body:
Servers, but within the Internet Server.

The first finding concerns the basic deployment pattern of legacy KIATIS. The system originated around
2006 as a simple file‑attachment bulletin board created by an Army computing unit and hosted on an
existing web server. It was used by approximately one hundred users, including staff at the 6·25 War Dead
Identification Agency, the Defense Ministry’s Investigation Headquarters, and various service‑branch
units. Conversations with working‑level staff at the MKIA confirm that this configuration, with KIATIS
running as an application on the Internet server and the Integrated Email Mail Server, remained in use until
at least December 2022.
This non‑dedicated deployment is documented in the new KIATIS project’s Request for Proposals
(RFP). An internal review of the draft RFP by the Agency, dated 11 September 2018, describes the “6·25
War Dead Integrated Information System” as “installed and operating on another system’s server.” The
final RFP published by the Public Procurement Service on 29 October 2018 contains the same description.
From the outset, therefore, KIATIS was not treated as a stand‑alone, securely segmented system; it was a
guest on servers whose primary function was something else.

sections.title: 3.1.1.2. Finding 2: Legacy KIATIS Was “Laundered” into the Integrated
sections.body:
Internet Mail Server

A second finding concerns the relocation of KIATIS into a new host environment. An “ordinary audit
result notification” issued by the MND’s Logistics Audit Office on 14 August 2018, regarding the new
KIATIS performance improvement project, describes the project as an effort to improve the “6·25 War
Dead Integrated Information System” developed in 2010, and identifies the related infrastructure as part
of the “All‑Forces Internet Integration Construction Project.”34 35 In effect, the underlying KIATIS
application was moved from its original web server into an integrated internet mail server environment.
This move functioned as a form of “server laundering”. The business name changed; the server
designation changed; but the core logic remained the same: an information system handling sensitive
defense‑related data continued to operate over the public internet, now under the label of an “integrated
mail server” rather than a “homepage server.” The new label obscured the continuity of the system’s
exposure and made it more difficult to trace the line back to the conditions that existed at the time of the
2016 hacking incident.

sections.title: 3.1.1.3. Finding 3: Legacy KIATIS Exchanged Data with Intranet (Defense
sections.body:
Network) Systems

A third finding relates to the system’s connectivity. A project plan drafted in August 2018 by the DCIA
project management team, and an accompanying MKIA review of the draft RFP, list “intranet homepage
linkage” as a key requirement. The new KIATIS was to be linked to the Agency’s intranet homepage and
to provide integrated views of investigation, excavation, identification, and family information.
This linkage implies that the legacy KIATIS, running on an internet‑facing server, was already
exchanging data with intranet‑based systems. In practice, this meant that data flowed between the public
internet and the defense intranet through the KIATIS host servers. The defense network was not isolated
from the internet; it was bridged through application‑level data exchange. For an external attacker who
had established a foothold on the internet‑facing server, such linkages would present attractive routes for
lateral movement into the defense domain.

sections.title: 3.1.1.4. Finding 4: Legacy KIATIS Ran on an Internet Homepage Server Using
sections.body:
Active‑X

The fourth finding concerns the application stack and client environment. In July 2018, the MKIA sent
a “request for cooperation in promoting the 6·25 War Dead Integrated Information System (KIATIS)
performance improvement project” to DCIA’s Administrative Informatization Division. In this document,
the MKIA explicitly lists as an expected benefit of the project the “elimination of currently used Active‑X
to remove security vulnerabilities.” The same document, and the final RFP, repeatedly emphasize the need
to “eliminate Active‑X,” “improve web standards compliance,” and “prohibit the use of Active‑X in the
new system.”
The security requirements section of the RFP further clarifies that KIATIS is treated as a web application
running on an internet homepage server. The combination of Active‑X components, browser‑based access,
34 The period for the Integrated Internet Construction Project for the Entire Military is documented as November
2018, to May 2019 (17 months). (Record p. 3470). The "Integrated Test Evaluation Results Report for the
Army-wide Internet Mail Integration Project" (May 2019, Record p. 5818) states the project plan approval date
as July 2018, and the project period as November 2018, to May 2019 (total 192 days). Only after
reviewing the complete documentation can a more accurate analysis and determination of potential manipulation
be made.
35 The evaluation committee chair for this project is Navy Commander C.T.G., who was my predecessor. While his
organizational assignment remained with the Administrative Information Division, he performed duties within the
Resource Management Division (Records pp. 5819, 3485, 3491).
and an internet‑facing host is significant. Active‑X controls have long been recognized as a major source
of client‑side vulnerabilities, and their use in public‑sector systems has been the subject of sustained
criticism. In this case, they formed part of a broader pattern in which an ostensibly web‑based interface
masked a deeper structural exposure: the direct, unauthenticated connection from client devices to the
database.
Testimony from the Agency’s project officer—who served as requirement originator, project manager,
project briefing officer, and evaluation committee member—confirms that KIATIS was operated on an
internet homepage server and that one of the project’s main goals was to rid the system of Active‑X. This
further underscores the degree to which the legacy configuration was understood, internally, to be both
outdated and insecure.

sections.title: 3.1.1.5. Finding 5: Legacy KIATIS Operated Without VPN or Database Access
sections.body:
Control

The fifth finding concerns the absence of basic security devices. Multiple sources—including written
statements from the MKIA’s project officer and evaluation committee members, as well as discussions
with the project manager of the contractor—indicate that KIATIS was used from MKIA offices and remote
locations without VPN. Users accessed the system as if they were using an ordinary website, but in reality,
they were connecting directly to the database.
The core structural deception lay in this duality. On the surface, users saw a browser window and
believed they were interacting with a standard web application. In fact, after loading a page in the browser,
an Active‑X control initiated a direct connection from the client machine to the database server, bypassing
any VPN or database access control system. The system thus behaved like a client–server application
masquerading as a web application. This configuration not only violated the DIDC’s own security
regulations, which require the use of SSL‑VPN for remote access, but also nullified essential protections
such as encrypted transport, strong authentication, and audit logging.

sections.title: 3.1.1.6. Finding 6: Direct Database Access from the Internet Without Security
sections.body:
Devices

A sixth finding, supported by detailed interviews and meeting records, concerns the actual usage
practices around KIATIS. In a July 2022 conversation, the MKIA’s project officer described how
excavation teams used KIATIS in the field. Team members carried mobile devices, such as Galaxy Note
smartphones, and accessed the system over the internet to retrieve and update data. They did so without
VPN or database access control; the path from their devices to the database server was effectively
unmediated by security appliances.
During a subsequent multi‑party discussion convened to consider how to normalize the new KIATIS
operation, 3637 the excavation team leader confirmed that, for many years, the unit had depended on this
remote, unsecured mode of access. This testimony, combined with the documentary record, supports a
picture in which KIATIS functioned as an internet‑exposed database application serving both office and
field users, with minimal security controls.

sections.title: 3.1.1.7. Joint Operation of KIATIS and Homepage Servers by the Same
sections.body:
Administrative Team

A seventh finding concerns organizational arrangements. Interrogation transcripts and organizational
charts dated December 2019 show that the Administrative Information Operations Team within DCIA’s
Administrative Informatization Division was responsible for both the “6·25 War Dead Integrated
Information System” and the Ministry’s internet and intranet homepages. A project labeled “defense
homepage improvement,”38 conducted from September 2016 to April 2017 and nominally described as
“maintenance” work39, in fact focused on remediating ten major security vulnerabilities on the homepage
server.
Subsequently, in May 2019, as part of the integrated internet mail server project, KIATIS was moved
from the homepage server to the integrated mail server.40 Responsibility for the mail server lay with a
different team within the division. Nonetheless, legacy KIATIS and its host environment had long been
under the care of a small group of civil servants and officers in the Administrative Information Operations
and Integration Teams. Audit trails, security review updates, and organizational charts identify specific
individuals by name, making it possible to trace who was responsible for the configuration and operation
of these systems over time.

An eighth finding emerges from the “technology application table” in the new KIATIS RFP. In the
security section of this table, which lists dozens of information protection technologies (including VPNs
and database access control systems), nearly all items are marked as “not applicable.” In response to my
inquiry, the Ministry of the Interior and Safety later confirmed that this was formally permissible because
the new KIATIS performance improvement project was categorized as a pure software development effort
that would reuse existing infrastructure.
However, the same table lists the underlying platform as a Unix/Linux server with a relational database.
For a reader who knows that legacy KIATIS was operated on an internet homepage server without
intervening security devices, the combination of “Unix/Linux relational database” and “not applicable” for
VPN and database access control implies a continuation of an internet‑based, lightly protected
architecture.
An earlier internal review of the draft RFP by the MKIA included a constraint stating that the technology
standards in the application table should be examined to ensure that they fit the project and that the system
should be implemented using open technologies not tied to specific proprietary solutions. This constraint
was removed in the final RFP drafted by DCIA. The removal suggests an intentional narrowing of the
formal design space to options that would preserve the existing, problematic architecture.

*** KIATIS: Killed In Action Total Information System
Synthesizing the eight findings and their corollaries, Layer 1 allows us to visualize the operation of
legacy KIATIS from 2007 to 2022 as shown in <Figure 5>. The system began as a bulletin‑board‑style file
attachment application hosted on an Army web server. It was then moved to DCIA’s homepage server and,
in 2014, migrated to DIDC 1st Center in Yongin. Throughout this period, it ran on internet‑facing servers,
first as part of a homepage configuration and later as part of an integrated internet mail server.
From 2007 to at least 2019, users accessed KIATIS from MKIA offices and the field via ordinary internet
connections, using browsers and Active‑X controls to reach the database directly. In 2019, the new KIATIS
performance improvement project was launched, and the system’s application codebase began to be
re‑implemented. But the underlying structural features—internet exposure, absence of VPN and database
access control, and data exchange with intranet systems—persisted well into 2021 and beyond.

3.1.1.10. Supplementary explanation and Rebuttal
The Military Prosecutor’s Office built its case around the claim that, in the September 2019 test
environment, the author and a junior officer violated DIDC security regulations by not using VPNs and
database access control, by requesting a firewall port opening that made the test environment “different”
from the supposed operational environment, and by failing to inform the evaluation committee of this
difference. As noted above, a single internal document suffices to dismantle this logic: a letter showing
that, in the new KIATIS environment, VPN and database access control was not in use from 27 August
2019 to at least 23 April 2021.
In other words, the configuration that prosecutors described as an “exceptional manipulation” in 2019
was, in fact, the standard configuration of KIATIS for an extended period before and after the test.
The legacy system had been operated without security devices from the beginning; the new system
continued this practice for years. To present this reality as the result of a project manager’s deliberate,
short‑term “deactivation” of security is to reverse cause and effect. It is a textbook example of shifting
responsibility for a structural, collective failure onto a single individual.
On the basis of the evidence presented, Layer 1 supports the following derived propositions. “Corollary 1.” Legacy KIATIS was operated for approximately fifteen years, from 2007 to 2022,
directly over the internet without VPN or database access control. This constitutes one of the most
serious and prolonged security violations in the history of the Korean armed forces. “Corollary 2.” Legacy KIATIS, as deployed on internet‑facing servers at DIDC 1st Center, was the
true root server exploited in the 2016 DIDC North Korean hacking incident. The system’s structural
vulnerabilities were not hypothetical; they were operationalized in an actual attack. “Corollary 3.” The primary motive behind the Defense Informatization Cartel’s organized
concealment and manipulation was to systematically remove or neutralize the evidence of KIATIS’s
fifteen‑year internet operation. Layer 1 thus reveals the “first button” in the chain of decisions that led
from a structural security failure to an APT‑style campaign against a whistleblower.

sections.title: 3.1.2. Background and Trajectory of the Active‑X Elimination Project
sections.body:
The Active‑X elimination project formally began with a “test and evaluation plan report for the
Defense Homepage Improvement Project” on 11 April 2017. Publicly, the project was presented as a
technical modernization effort aimed at improving web standards compliance, addressing security
vulnerabilities in Active‑X components, and enhancing user convenience.
However, a chronological analysis of project documents and regulatory amendments reveals a deeper
layer. Shortly after the 2016 hacking incident, the MND initiated a wave of Active‑X removal and
homepage improvement activities. In August 2017, the Ministry circulated a request for review of the
2017 first‑half Active‑X elimination results and the 2018–2020 elimination plan. Around the same time,
it issued cooperation requests for the distribution of new client components on the On‑nara system for
the Ministry and the Joint Chiefs of Staff.
These activities coincided with a sequence of amendments to the Defense Informatization Directive:
No. 2129 (5 February 2018), No. 2263 (26 February 2019), and No. 2398 (13 February 2020). While
framed as technical updates, these amendments also had the effect of providing ex post legal cover for
practices that had previously been in violation of existing rules. The timing suggests that the Active‑X
elimination project served not only to improve security but also to “erase technical traces” of past
configurations and to normalize, in regulatory language, configurations that had been irregular.
From this perspective, the elimination of Active‑X was a double‑edged tool. On one edge, it addressed
genuine security concerns associated with outdated client‑side components. On the other edge, it
facilitated the removal of logs, configurations, and code paths that would have made it easier to
reconstruct how KIATIS had been connected and operated at the time of the 2016 hacking incident.

sections.title: 3.1.3. Intentional removal and concealment of the legacy KIATIS history
sections.body:
This layer’s most serious implications emerge from a combination of testimony, interrogation records,
and project documents. Conversations with the MKIA’s project officer confirm that KIATIS was first
operated at DCIA and later moved to DIDC 1st Center in Yongin. Technical literature and threat reports
on Active‑X attacks show that the vulnerabilities inherent in such components had been widely known
since at least the late 2000s.
In light of this knowledge, the decision to continue operating KIATIS as an internet‑exposed Active‑X
system without VPN or database access control is difficult to explain as mere negligence. More likely,
it reflects a pattern of organizational choices in which the convenience of existing workflows and the
avoidance of costly redesign were systematically prioritized over compliance and security.
After the 2016 hacking incident, the Ministry faced a fork in the road. One path would have involved
acknowledging that a critical defense system had been built and operated in violation of basic security
rules, investigating how this had occurred, and holding responsible actors accountable. The other path,
which was chosen, involved “gradual erasure and reinterpretation”.

> 📊 **Figure 6 — Connection to the Old KIATIS in the Active-X Removal Project**

![Figure_6_Old_KIATIS_ActiveX_Removal_Project.png](./images/Figure_6_Old_KIATIS_ActiveX_Removal_Project.png)

From 2016 onward, technical measures such as Active‑X elimination and “maintenance” projects
were used to change the surface appearance of systems while leaving the underlying control gaps in
place. In parallel, the Defense Informatization Directive was amended in ways that, when read forward,
could be construed as tightening procedures, but when read backward functioned to “retroactively
legalize” practices that had been irregular.
Between 2018 and 2022, the new KIATIS performance improvement project provided a vehicle for
“replacing” the legacy system in a way that would allow its traces to disappear. The legacy application
was recoded, rehosted, and eventually decommissioned, but the narrative surrounding its operation was
never publicly corrected. Internally, the system’s past was increasingly described in terms that
minimized its exposure and emphasized the supposed continuity of security controls.

Personnel moves mirrored this process. Key individuals associated with the legacy system’s design,
approval, and operation were transferred to other posts, promoted, or moved out of the organization.
Technical staff retired or shifted to the private sector. Audit personnel rotated to other assignments. The

One document, a 2019–2020 Active‑X elimination plan review by the Informatization Planning
Office, takes the date of 26 October 2018—the submission date of the new KIATIS RFP to the Public
Procurement Service—as a reference point for defining which systems are within scope of the
elimination project. The implicit message is that Active‑X issues that surface after that date belong to
the future and, by implication, are not attributable to earlier design and oversight decisions.

sections.title: 3.1.4. Structural Peculiarities of Legacy KIATIS Operation
sections.body:
The most striking structural peculiarity of legacy KIATIS is that it was, in effect, a “pseudo web
system”. At the user interface level, it appeared to be a standard web application accessed via a browser.
At the network and application levels, it behaved as a client–server system in which an Active‑X
component on the client machine established a direct, unencrypted, and poorly authenticated connection
to the database server.
From 2007 until the system’s replacement in 2022, all users accessed the database directly from the
internet without VPN. The technical implications are stark. Communications were unencrypted; user
authentication and authorization were weak; audit trails were incomplete or easily circumvented; and
the system was highly vulnerable to man‑in‑the‑middle attacks, network sniffing, and other APT‑style
techniques.
This configuration did not persist for fifteen years by accident. As the DIDC’s own regulations make
clear, remote access to information systems is supposed to occur via SSL‑VPN, with robust access
control and logging. The repeated failure to apply these rules to KIATIS, even after the 2016 hacking
incident, and even after the system was “washed” into the integrated mail server, points to a pattern of
“deliberate tolerance of non‑compliance”.

sections.title: 3.1.5. Closing the First Layer
sections.body:
By reconstructing the architecture, deployment, and usage of legacy KIATIS, Layer 1 accomplishes
three tasks. It proves that KIATIS was operated for fifteen years in a manner that made it an ideal entry
point for APT‑style attacks; it shows that this configuration was structurally bound up with the 2016
DIDC hacking incident; and it documents the first steps by which the Defense Informatization Cartel
began to remove and obscure this history through technical projects and regulatory amendments.
These findings form the indispensable foundation for the subsequent layers. Without understanding
what KIATIS was, how it was actually operated, and why its existence was intolerable for those
responsible, it is impossible to understand why a new project was designed as it was, why tests were
manipulated, why a false abuse complaint was launched, or why the Military Prosecutor’s Office
devoted so much energy to portraying a whistleblower as a criminal.
3.2.

Second Layer: Governance of the New KIATIS Project and
Manipulation of an Officer’s Record

Layer 2 analyzes the governance of the new KIATIS performance improvement project. It shows that
the Ministry of National Defense (MND) and DCIA violated the Defense Informatization Directive by
illegally divesting the MND of its controlling‑MKIA role, by misclassifying the project as a delegated
project, and by manipulating project documents in order to shift responsibility away from themselves
and toward the user MKIA and, ultimately, toward a single officer. It also shows how the career record
and functional role of that officer were deliberately engineered to support future blame‑shifting.

sections.title: 3.2.1. Manipulation of Project Approval, Project Plan, and Request for Proposals
3.2.1.1. Systematic Violation of the Defense Informatization Directive
sections.body:
The starting point is the status of KIATIS under the Defense Informatization Directive. Legacy
KIATIS was, without doubt, an MND‑controlled system. It was hosted at DCIA from 2007 until its
migration to DIDC 1st Center in 2014; its security review records appear in DCIA’s security
countermeasure review documents from at least 2015 onward; and a 17 June 2021 budget document
for the 2021–2022 Administrative Informatization Division maintenance projects list KIATIS among
“information systems operated by the MND headquarters.” KIATIS was thus a Ministry system and its
improvement was, by definition, a Ministry‑controlled project.
The new KIATIS performance improvement project was also, by regulatory definition, an
MND‑controlled project. Under Article 10(4) of the Defense Informatization Directive, projects related
to information systems operated by the MND fall under the Ministry’s control. Article 11(2) makes clear
that such projects cannot be treated as delegated projects41; only certain categories of lower‑tier projects
qualify for delegation. Moreover, the new KIATIS project had the status of a “national agenda project”

41 "Article 58 (Principles for Conducting Test and Evaluation) ① When introducing commercial information and
communications products, test and evaluation shall be replaced by evaluation and inspection (acceptance testing) for
product selection. However, commercial information and communications products introduced as part of an information
system construction project may be integrated into developed business application software and undergo operational test
and evaluation. ② The test evaluation procedures for Ministry of National Defense control projects shall follow the
provisions of Articles 59 through Development test evaluation and operational test evaluation shall be conducted
separately as a principle; however, they may be conducted simultaneously with the approval of the project control agency
when necessary. ③ For agency-delegated projects, information system construction projects with software development
costs under 500 million won, and projects where test evaluation was delegated at the project plan approval stage pursuant
to Article Articles 59 through 64 shall apply mutatis mutandis. Test evaluation shall be conducted according to detailed
procedures established by the relevant agency, and the results shall be reported to the project control agency. ④ Projects
subject to Paragraph 3 may conduct development test evaluations and operational test evaluations simultaneously within
the operational test evaluation environment under the supervision of the project management agency and the project
managing agency. ⑤ When establishing test evaluation plans, the project management agency and project management
organization shall prepare evaluation plans concerning interoperability and information protection by applying Chapter
Interoperability Management, and separate directives, and shall request test evaluations from agencies entrusted with tasks
in the relevant fields or specialized agencies.⑥ The project management agency, project executive agency, and service
providers (including suppliers) shall support all matters related to the test evaluation."
under the administration’s list of one hundred key government tasks, further reinforcing its centrality
and the requirement of ministerial control.
Accordingly, until at least 12 August 2021, when a later revision of the Directive (No. 2576) came
into force, the new KIATIS project should have been conducted under the MND’s control, including a
strict separation between development testing and operational testing as required by Article 58(2) of
Directive No. 2129 (5 February 2018). The provision in Article 58(4) that allows development and
operational testing to be conducted jointly in the operational environment applies only to delegated
projects that meet specific criteria; it could not lawfully be applied to the new KIATIS project.
Even if one were to argue, contra the Directive, that some form of integrated testing was unavoidable,
such a departure from the default rule would still have required explicit approval by the controlling
agency, the MND’s Informatization Planning Office. No such approval was ever issued. The only date
that appears in the relevant documents—9 July 2018, the date of the MKIA’s request for cooperation
in promoting the KIATIS performance improvement project—is later recycled as the MND project
approval date in an August 2019 test plan report signed by the DCIA director, from which the project
management team leader (the author) was excluded.
Despite repeated efforts, the author was unable to locate any genuine MND approval document for
the project. The records instead convey the impression that the MKIA itself, rather than the MND, had
“approved” the project, and that this internal approval was retroactively treated as if it were a
Ministry‑level control decision. The most plausible reading is that the Informatization Planning Office,
from the stage of project initiation through to the test phase, simply “did not perform its
controlling‑MKIA duties” in relation to KIATIS.
This inference is reinforced by the content of the DCIA project plan and reporting documents. On 7
August 2018, the DCIA division director reported the “KIATIS Performance Improvement Project Plan”
to the DCIA director, describing the “controlling and implementing agency” as the 6·25 MKIA and
classifying the project as an “agency‑delegated project.” An undated project plan prepared around the
same time likewise designates the MKIA as both controlling and implementing agency. In other words,
DCIA and the MKIA agreed—contrary to the Directive and without genuine Ministry approval—to treat
a Ministry‑controlled, national‑agenda project as if it were a delegated project controlled by the user
agency.
This is not a minor procedural irregularity. Under the Directive, only the MND’s Informatization
Planning Office may perform the four core functions of a controlling agency: reviewing and deciding
project requirements, reviewing and reflecting medium‑term plans and budgets, approving project plans,
and approving test and evaluation plans and results. To assign these functions to a user organization that
neither has nor can be granted such authority is to exceed the Ministry’s own legal powers. It is, in effect,
to rewrite the Directive in practice while leaving its text nominally intact.

sections.title: 3.2.1.2. Illegal Transfer of Control and Responsibility to the User MKIA
sections.body:
The illegal transfer of control becomes even clearer when compared with another project conducted
by the same division in the same period. From April 2017 to January 2019, the Administrative
Informatization Division implemented a “Defense Organization and Personnel Information System
Advancement Project.” In that project, the controlling MKIA was the MND Informatization Planning
Office, the user MKIA was the MND’s Organization and Personnel Directorate, and the managing
MKIA was DCIA. The Directive’s division of roles was faithfully observed: the controlling MKIA
performed all four core functions, including separate approvals for development and operational tests.
By contrast, in the new KIATIS project, all three key documents—the project plan (dated August
2018), the project plan report (7 August 2018), and the final RFP (29 October 2018)—designate the 6·25
MKIA as both controlling and implementing agency. The Ministry is absent. The MKIA, which
originally played the role of requirement originator and user, is elevated on paper to a dual‑cap role
(control plus implementation) that it has neither the mandate nor the capacity to fulfill as shown in the

This arrangement served several purposes. For the MND, it created a buffer against accountability: if
the project later encountered problems, the Ministry could point to the formal documents and claim that

status and control, while in reality much of the substantive project management and technical
