sections.title: 3.2.1.3. Intentional Underfunding of Essential Software and Hardware Requirements
sections.body:
45 This document states the new KIATIS performance improvement project is a <National Policy Task> (Record p. 1064).
Furthermore, the project manager is listed as the Administrative Operations Team (Team Leader L.S.I.) of the
Administrative Informationization Division. That is, looking at the National Assembly approval process, it appears this
project had only recently been transferred to the Planning Team, as it was approved by Planning Team Leader Navy
Commander C.T.G. and reviewed by Operations Team Leader L.S.I. For reference on this, please see Record 11107.
A further aspect of the manipulation concerns the project’s budget and scope. The new KIATIS
project, as managed by DCIA, was a pure software development service contract under the National
Contract Act, with a development budget of 6.25 billion won. This amount corresponded to the cost
estimate determined by the Public Procurement Service, based on the software requirements specified
by the Agency.
However, the RFP’s technical and security requirements reveal a tension. On the one hand, they call
for significant functional improvements, including integration of multiple data domains and elimination
of Active‑X. On the other hand, the technology application table marks key security infrastructure
elements—VPN, database access control, and other information protection assets—as “not applicable,”
on the grounds that the project is “software only.”
This combination suggests that the project was underfunded relative to its true needs. The system
was expected to deliver enhanced functionality and security, but without dedicated funding for the
necessary infrastructure changes at DIDC and DCIA. As later layers show, the evaluation committee’s
decision to adopt eighty additional requirements during the test phase effectively doubled the functional
scope, while the budget remained fixed. This mismatch between expectations and resources created
fertile ground for later accusations of “poor development,” even though the underlying cause lay in
decisions made by the project’s initiators and controllers.
In sum, the manipulation of project approvals, governance roles, and budgets in Layer 2 laid the
groundwork for the later portrayal of the project as a delegated, underperforming effort whose failures
could be blamed on those at the operational edge rather than on those who had designed an impossible
mandate.

sections.title: 3.2.2. Illegal Reclassification as a Delegated Project and Evasion of MND
sections.body:
Approval
A particularly revealing document in this layer is a memorandum titled “Request for Review of the
Plan to Simplify Information System Construction Test and Evaluation Procedures,” produced by the
Informatization Planning Office and sent to DCIA on 2 September 2019—the very day the integrated
development and operational test for new KIATIS began. The memorandum proposes to “omit the
procedures for project plan approval and test and evaluation plan and result approval” by the controlling
agency, and it states that if no objections are raised, agreement with the plan will be assumed.
This document effectively “declares that the MND will not exercise its controlling‑agency functions”
for the KIATIS project, particularly with respect to test and evaluation. It is a direct violation of Article
11 of the Directive, which obliges the controlling agency to approve project plans and test and evaluation
plans and results. It is also procedurally suspect: the memorandum was issued on the first day of the test,
leaving no real opportunity for substantive review, and it contains a glaring temporal inconsistency, in
that the attachment it purports to transmit bears a report date later than the memorandum itself.
The Logistics Audit Office’s earlier “ordinary audit result notification” of 14 August 2018 displays a
similar pattern of complicity. In that document, the auditors accept without objection the classification
of the KIATIS project as an “agency‑delegated project” with the 6·25 agency as both controlling and
implementing agency. This acceptance is difficult to reconcile with the Directive but easy to understand
if one assumes that the auditors are part of the same network as the Informatization Planning Office and
DCIA.
The same notification also contains an important clue about the infrastructure hosting KIATIS. It
identifies the “All‑Forces Internet Integrated Mail Project” as the business title under which the system’s
host servers are managed. This confirms that KIATIS was running within the integrated internet mail
server cluster at DIDC 1st Center; it also shows that the auditors knew this and chose not to question
the arrangement.
Additional manipulations include the apparent cancellation and re‑announcement of the RFP without
proper approval by the DCIA director, and the holding of a project briefing by the MKIA’s project
officer, who presented himself as belonging to DCIA in order to satisfy the Directive’s requirement that
the managing MKIA conduct the briefing. These acts are more than clerical irregularities; they are
deliberate distortions of roles and procedures designed to create a record that would later support the
claim that the project was controlled and implemented by the Agency, with DCIA and the MND acting
only in a supporting capacity.

sections.title: 3.2.3. Deliberate Manipulation of an Officer’s Career Record and Role
sections.body:
The final component of Layer 2 concerns the manipulation of an individual officer’s assignment
history,46understood here as the combination of formal career record and demonstrated professional
capability. The author’s postings within DCIA were changed twice during his tenure, in ways that appear
minor on the surface but that, in retrospect, align closely with the Cartel’s evolving strategy.
One noteworthy pattern is the concentration of key KIATIS roles in the hands of a small group of
Navy officers. A Navy commander, serving as project management team leader before the author’s
transfer, was both involved in arranging the informal delegation of control to the MKIA and later
appointed as evaluation committee chair for the 2019 integrated development and operational test of the
integrated mail server at DIDC 1st Center. A Navy lieutenant was directly involved in canceling and
re‑announcing the RFP without proper approvals. Another Navy lieutenant, serving as project staff
officer at DCIA, bypassed the project management team leader to report the test plan directly to the
director and, as evaluation committee secretary, played a central role in introducing the eighty additional
requirements.
Most significant, however, is the persistent involvement of two civilian officials: Section Chief J.Y.I.
and Administrative Officer J.Y.N. From 2017, during the Active‑X elimination project and the early
stages of planning for the new KIATIS project, they worked together in the Informatization Planning
Office. In January and February 2021, both were transferred to DCIA, to the very division and team

46 For an explanation of this, please refer to "Level 5: (False) Power Abuse Report Followed by Forced Solitary Confinement
and Reputation Terrorism."
responsible for KIATIS. At the Ministry, they had been involved in drafting and amending the Directive
and in shaping project classifications; at DCIA, they continued to play similar roles, now closer to the
operational coalface.
Their fingerprints can be seen on numerous key documents: the project plan that misclassified KIATIS
as an agency‑delegated project; the test and evaluation plans and reports that omitted the author from
decision‑making roles; the internal memoranda that sought to omit MND approvals; and later, the
analyses and submissions to the Military Prosecutor’s Office that framed the author as the central culprit.
They also appear in documents concerning the “GIS server” and “GIS function addition,” in which
Director Jeong argued that the project had been underfunded and that additional infrastructure was
needed to address performance issues. This is consistent with the MKIA project officer’s testimony that
the MND had “consciously underfunded” the original project by omitting key infrastructure elements at
the requirement‑definition stage.
In this way, the manipulation of the author’s role and record was embedded in a broader pattern of
personnel and document management. His appointment as project management team leader placed him
at the center of the project just as its most visible milestones approached, but his exclusion from key
approvals and his lack of control over critical decisions left him exposed. At the same time, those who
had overseen the legacy system’s configuration and the project’s initial design remained in positions
from which they could shape narratives and documents, but out of the direct line of accountability.
Taken together, the elements analyzed in Layer 2—misclassification of the project, illegal transfer of
control, evasion of MND approvals, underfunding of essential requirements, and manipulation of an
officer’s role and record—constitute the governance framework of the Cartel’s scheme. This
framework made it possible, in later layers, to portray normal project management actions as crimes, to
insulate key decision‑makers from scrutiny, and to turn a systemic failure into a personal tragedy.
3.3.

Third Layer: Assignment to DCIA and the Management and
Completion of the Software Development Project

Layer 3 covers the period from 3 December 2018 to 18 October 2019, during which the author was
assigned to the Defense Computing Information Agency (DCIA) and served as software development
project manager for the new KIATIS performance improvement project. This layer has two main
purposes. First, it demonstrates that the author’s work during this period was normal, diligent, and
consistent with his formal responsibilities, and that he did not participate in, nor could he have controlled,
the test and evaluation process that was later used as the basis for criminal accusations. Second, it shows
how, during this same period, the Defense Informatization Cartel prepared for later manipulations by
excluding the author from key decisions, reassigning duties, and appropriating his ideas, thereby creating
the conditions for his future scapegoating.

sections.title: 3.3.1. Manipulations During the Initiation and Completion of Software Project
sections.body:
Management
The first set of evidence in this layer concerns the circumstances of the author’s posting and the
configuration of roles around him. Primary documents and statements reveal several anomalies and
patterns that are essential to understanding how he was positioned.

sections.title: 3.3.1.1. Anomalous Vacancy and Unrecorded Change in the Project Management
sections.body:
Position

When the author was assigned to DCIA on 3 December 2018, he found that the project management
team leader post for the KIATIS project had been vacant for approximately two months. In normal
personnel practice, especially for a national‑agenda project of strategic importance, such a key role
would not be left unfilled. The prolonged vacancy is therefore unlikely to have been accidental; it
appears to have been a preparatory measure to ensure that the author could be inserted into a position
whose contours had been shaped without his involvement.
There was also a discrepancy between the author’s formal organizational placement and his actual
work assignment. On paper, his billet belonged to the Logistics Branch of the Resource Informatization
Division. In practice, however, he was placed in the Administrative Informatization Division’s general
affairs and planning team, where he assumed responsibility for the KIATIS project and other
administrative systems. This misalignment between formal billet and actual duties is significant. It
created room for later manipulation of his personnel record, allowing his role in the project to be
emphasized or minimized as convenient, and it suggests an intention to make his responsibility appear
ambiguous and thus more easily redirected.

sections.title: 3.3.1.2. Deliberate Reassignment of KIATIS Responsibilities Before the Author’s
sections.body:
Arrival
Before the author’s transfer, responsibility for KIATIS had rested with the Administrative Information
Operations Team. Shortly before his arrival, this responsibility was reassigned to the planning team to
which he would be attached. Records and testimony indicate that this was not a routine organizational
adjustment but a deliberate reshuffling.
The effect of this reassignment was to separate the project management function from the repository
of institutional memory about KIATIS. The historical operational information and knowledge of
security vulnerabilities remained within the operations team, while the author, as the new project
management team leader, inherited the formal responsibility for the new KIATIS project without being
given access to critical background information. In particular, information about the legacy system’s
long‑standing operation over the internet without VPN and database access control remained confined
to those who had been operating and maintaining the system.
As Layer 4 later shows, this separation was part of a broader pattern designed to prevent the author
from understanding the full extent of the system’s structural vulnerabilities. If he lacked that
understanding, then any problems emerging during testing could more easily be portrayed as the result
of his own failings, rather than as the legacy of fifteen years of non‑compliant operation.

sections.title: 3.3.2. Acting as Deputy Director, General Coordinator, and Manager of Other
sections.body:
Projects
A second set of evidence concerns the breadth and nature of the author’s duties at DCIA. These duties
demonstrate that he was entrusted with substantial responsibility and that he performed it in a manner
consistent with expectations for a senior officer.
Three days after his posting, on 6 December 2018, he signed, by delegated authority, a formal
document titled “Payment Request for Replacement of Old Equipment in the e‑System,” in lieu of the
division director. From that point until his isolation in February 2022, he regularly performed the
functions of deputy director and general coordinator in the Administrative Informatization Division.
He reviewed daily security summaries and signed them on behalf of the division director, attended
director‑level meetings as the division’s representative, and coordinated with other divisions and
external organizations.
In his capacity as project management team leader, the author was responsible for seven of the
fourteen projects assigned to the Administrative Informatization Division in 2018–2019. These projects
covered a wide range of administrative systems. Simultaneously, he served as general coordinator,
assisting in the oversight of the remaining projects and supporting the division director in strategic
planning. This combination of roles indicates a high degree of trust in his professional competence and
judgment.
The interrogation transcripts and internal conversations with a later division director confirm that the
author’s work in these roles was seen, at the time, as competent and valuable. There is no evidence in
the contemporaneous record that his performance was considered deficient. This is important because it
underscores the contrast between the positive internal assessment of his work during the period of
Layer 3 and the later narrative of incompetence and misconduct constructed by the Cartel in Layers
5 and

sections.title: 3.3.3. Patterns of Conduct by Civil Servants and the Misappropriation of
sections.body:
the Author’s Ideas
A third set of evidence concerns the behavior of certain civil servants and senior officers around the
author, which reveals both a pattern of disrespect and marginalization and a pattern of appropriating
his intellectual contributions.

sections.title: 3.3.3.1. Denigration of Officers by the DCIA Director
sections.body:
Shortly after the author’s assignment to DCIA, several junior officers—lieutenants and captains—
approached him to complain about repeated disparaging remarks by the DCIA director. According to
their accounts, the director frequently stated in meetings that “non‑commissioned officers are better than
officers,” remarks that they experienced as humiliating and demoralizing.
At first, the author found these reports hard to believe. However, several months later, during a
director’s meeting at which he was present, the director made precisely the same statement in front of
him. The author publicly challenged the remark, arguing that it was inappropriate and damaging to the
dignity of commissioned officers. A debate ensued in full view of the other participants and lasted for
roughly twenty minutes. The director later referred to this confrontation in a handwritten note he left for
the author before his retirement.
These episodes are not just anecdotes. They indicate an environment in which officers, including those
in critical roles such as the KIATIS project management team leader, could be openly disparaged by
their superiors. In such an environment, an officer who raised uncomfortable questions—such as the
author’s questions about KIATIS’s architecture and the governance of the project—could be more easily
marginalized and painted as “difficult” or “troublesome.” The director’s denigration of officers thus
functioned as a social backdrop for the later isolation and discrediting of the author.

sections.title: 3.3.3.2. Misappropriation and Commercialization of the Author’s Mobile Phone
sections.body:
Security Architecture

Another revealing strand concerns the “personal mobile phone security control system” for enlisted
soldiers. On 1 July 2020, the MND implemented a policy allowing conscripts to use personal mobile
phones after duty hours. The supporting security system for this policy—the “personal mobile phone
security control system”—was developed and implemented as a project managed by the Administrative
Informatization Division at DCIA between October 2019 and March 2020.
In early 2019, during a director’s meeting at DCIA, the question of how to design a secure architecture
for soldier mobile phone use was raised. Despite the fact that this issue did not fall within his formal
project portfolio, the author was pressed by civil servants to attend related meetings at the MND and to
provide input. The division director subsequently asked him to design a security architecture for
controlling personal mobile phones.
This was not a trivial request. The author had been working on related questions since at least 2013,
when he served at Army Headquarters and had begun to study how to prevent or contain damage from
potential hacks of the Army’s internet servers. His research had produced several papers, including “A
Study on Implementing a Mobile Service Architecture Resilient to Hacking” (June 2016). Drawing on
this body of work, he spent more than a month developing a one‑page architectural draft titled
“Preliminary Architecture for Unit‑Level Security Control of Personal Mobile Phones” (6 March 2019,
Version 1.6), which he submitted to the director.
After this submission, the author heard little more about the matter until, after the first phase of the
project had been completed, he overheard the director and a team leader discussing the possibility of
filing a patent on the design. The implication was unmistakable: they were considering claiming
ownership of an architecture that had been conceived and designed by the author on the basis of his
years of research.
The budget for the first phase of the mobile phone security project was approximately 2.59 billion
won, with further phases planned. The author has reason to believe that his design was used as the basis
for this project and that civil servants attempted to present it as their own innovative work, potentially
in collaboration with a favored contractor. Although definitive proof of this appropriation would require
additional investigation, the available evidence strongly suggests that the author’s intellectual work was
exploited without acknowledgment or proper credit.

sections.title: 3.3.3.3. Replacement as Evaluation Committee Chair After Raising Concerns
sections.body:
The author’s relationship with the DCIA leadership was further strained by his insistence on proper
standards in other projects. On one occasion, he was appointed chair of an evaluation committee for a
personal computer procurement project. During the evaluation, he raised concerns about the proposed
specifications and their alignment with actual needs, arguing that certain configurations were
inappropriate. As a result, the evaluation process was terminated and later reconvened with a different
chair.
Similarly, in the context of a major 200‑billion‑won “Defense Personnel Information System
Advancement Project,” the author participated in several meetings as general coordinator and raised
questions about the choice of development tools, the necessity of specific requirements, and the overall
coherence of the design. He went so far as to write on the whiteboard to illustrate his concerns. After
these interventions, he was no longer invited to related meetings.
These episodes demonstrate that the author was fulfilling his professional duty by scrutinizing large
projects and raising concerns about waste and misdesign. At the same time, they likely marked him, in
the eyes of certain civil servants and managers, as an unwelcome critic. This reputation made it easier,
later, to justify his exclusion from key decisions and to frame him as an obstacle rather than as an asset.
3.3.3.4.

Irregularities in the M‑KISS47I Project and Further Evidence of

The author’s experience with the M‑KISS (a military knowledge and information sharing system)
project further illustrates the pattern of irregularities and collusion between DCIA and MND
departments. After a reorganization in 2021, all operational tasks for four teams were consolidated into
a single team, while the author’s team was designated as the “planning and operations team.”
The author produced an analytical report titled “Improvement Plan for Roles and Procedures in the
M‑KISS System” (17 March 2021), in which he pointed out that, despite an annual maintenance contract
of 1.2 billion won with a private company, the bulk of critical operational tasks were being performed
by DCIA staff rather than by the contractor. The report recommended clear redefinition of roles among
the MND’s Human Resources Development Directorate, DCIA, and the contractor.
Shortly thereafter, the MND’s Human Resources Development Directorate issued a notice about a
“M‑KISS Utilization Promotion Workshop” (22 August 2019). The notice was addressed not to the team
that actually managed M‑KISS operations, but to another team—the “AI Convergence Systems
Development Team”—that had inherited the label of the former “Administrative Operations Team.”
The team leader annotated the notice with the comment that “since this is not a system managed by our
institute and we are only in a support relationship, there is no need to attend at this time,” obtained the
division director’s approval, and marked the notice for circulation to a junior officer on the author’s
team.
This episode suggests a pattern of collusion between certain DCIA managers and MND departments:
responsibilities and appearances were manipulated to create plausible deniability and to offload
inconvenient work onto those least able to resist. The junior officer in question—who would later file
the false abuse complaint against the author—figured prominently in the M‑KISS irregularities, and her
interactions with MND officials included incidents in which an MND civil servant, frustrated by her
behavior, stormed out of a meeting with the author.
The author’s detailed rebuttals to her later abuse allegations, contained in documents such as
“Expected Claims by First Lieutenant (later Captain) Yu Eun‑ji and Key Points of Rebuttal” and
“Proving Non‑Liability in the Abuse Investigation,” show that he had meticulously documented these
patterns and their implications. From the perspective of the Cartel, this made him not only a technical
and legal threat in the KIATIS case but also a witness to broader dysfunctions in defense informatization
projects.

and liberal arts content and operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year (information from internet search).

sections.title: 3.3.4. Risk‑Control Measures Initiated by the Project Management Team
sections.body:
Leader
The author understood that the project was, in principle, a pure software development project under
the National Contract Act. This gave him initial confidence: software‑only projects are, in many respects,
easier to define and control than projects involving large hardware or infrastructure components. At the
same time, he was concerned that the Ministry had improperly assigned the controlling‑agency role to
the 6·25 Agency, a user organization with limited experience in project control.
Drawing on three decades of experience in information and communications technology roles at Army
Headquarters, Combined Forces Command, the Central Procurement and Finance Commands, and
elsewhere, the author identified three main categories of risk and established corresponding “braking
mechanisms” at the outset of his tenure.
First, he sought to ensure that clear criteria and procedures would govern each phase of the project,
from requirements analysis to design, implementation, testing, and acceptance. He insisted on
documenting decision points and on aligning them with the Directive and the National Contract Act.
Second, he attempted to intervene directly at key decision moments, particularly where project
scope, quality criteria, or evaluation methods were being defined. His aim was to prevent ad hoc
expansions of scope or informal relaxations of standards that could later be used to argue that the project
had failed to meet ill‑defined expectations.
Third, he worked to identify potential risk factors in advance—including underfunding of
infrastructure, ambiguities in governance roles, and the persistence of legacy configurations—and to
develop contingency plans for addressing them.
At the time, the author did not imagine that the Cartel would attempt to manipulate the “base
environment” of the system—a domain that properly belongs to DIDC and the Informatization Planning
Office—as a means of staging a test failure and attributing it to project management. From his
perspective, it was self‑evident that the new KIATIS performance improvement project was
software‑only48 and that the base environment was the responsibility of other organizations. It was only

by DIDC to the Administrative Informatization Division of the DCIA " 2018 Information Resources Acquisition and

Information on the 2018 Defense Information System Development Plan." When the Ministry of National Defense revised
its directives starting in 2018, the section modified regarding information infrastructure pertains to matters related to the
new KIATIS project. For example, the VPN OTP card that became an issue starting in May 2019 was not unused during
the new KIATIS test evaluation period from September 2 to September 2019, but was used by the MKIA only after
April 2021. To elaborate, this clearly demonstrates that the Ministry of National Defense Prosecutor's Office's search
and seizure, and investigation—initiated against me as a suspect on six charges alleging manipulation of the September
2019 test evaluation environment—were entirely false. In fact, this single sentence alone proves the military prosecution's
targeted investigation and renders useless the logic built over a long period through collusion with the defense
informatization cartel. This argument serves as a <Notice> announcing the start of my decisive counterattack to cut down
those vicious individuals.

sections.title: 3.3.5. Closing the Third Layer
sections.body:
The core findings of Layer 3 can be summarized as follows.
During the period from December 2018 to October 2019, the author performed his duties as project
management team leader for the new KIATIS project, as deputy division director, and as general
coordinator for multiple projects in a normal and diligent manner. He was not a member of the test
and evaluation committee, did not approve the integrated test plan, and did not hold a position from
which he could have unilaterally designed or manipulated the test environment.
At the same time, the Defense Informatization Cartel used the period to prepare for later manipulations.
It changed his billet and separated his formal and actual duties, reassigned KIATIS responsibilities away
from the operations team that held critical information, appropriated his intellectual work for other
projects, and responded to his efforts to enforce proper standards by excluding him from sensitive roles.
Layer 3 thus exposes a deep contradiction. On the one hand, the author’s conduct during this period
embodies the professional norms of a senior IT and cybersecurity officer: he managed projects, raised
concerns about flawed designs, and attempted to impose structure and discipline. On the other hand,
these very qualities made him a threat to those who had overseen and benefited from irregular
arrangements. The Cartel’s subsequent decision to criminalize his role in the KIATIS project must be
understood against this backdrop of normal professional performance coupled with systematic
organizational marginalization.
3.4.

Fourth Layer: Organizational Manipulation of the New KIATIS
Development and Operational Test, KIDA’s Evaluation Research Fraud,
and Regulatory Engineering

Layer 4 is the structural core of the seven‑layer proof system. It analyzes how the Defense
Informatization Cartel (i) designed and conducted an integrated development and operational test for
the new KIATIS in a way that concealed the true operational environment, (ii) collaborated with the
Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA) to produce an “evaluation improvement” study that
academically legitimized this manipulation, and (iii) engineered amendments to the Defense
Informatization Directive so that the manipulated practice could later be presented as compliant with
“updated” rules.

sections.title: 3.4.1. The Normative Test Environment Under the Defense Informatization
sections.body:
Directive49
Under the Defense Informatization Directive (No. 2129 of 5 February 2018 and its immediate
successors), the basic principle of test and evaluation for defense IT systems is clear. Article 57
distinguishes between development testing and operational testing. Development testing is to be
conducted under the responsibility of the managing agency (DCIA, in this case), to verify that the system
meets its functional specifications. Operational testing is to be conducted under the responsibility of the
user agency, MKIA in the actual operational environment, to determine whether the system is
suitable for real‑world use.
Article 58(2) states that, for systems that improve existing capabilities—such as the new KIATIS
performance improvement project—development and operational testing are to be separated. Article
58(4) provides a narrow exception, allowing integrated development and operational testing in the
operational environment for certain delegated projects that meet specific conditions. As shown in Layer these conditions did not apply to KIATIS, which was a Ministry‑controlled, national‑agenda project,
not an agency‑delegated project.
If these rules had been followed, the new KIATIS project would have proceeded as follows. DCIA
would have conducted development testing in its own environment to verify the software’s functionality
and performance. The 6·25 Agency, as user, would then have conducted operational testing in its actual
operational environment, which, given the nature of the system as a performance improvement to an
existing capability, would have meant a carefully controlled environment that faithfully reflected the
conditions under which KIATIS was used in the field. The MND Informatization Planning Office, as
controlling agency, would have separately approved the plans and results of both phases.

This refers to the Defense Informationization Work Directive (No. 2129, February 2018). The Defense
Informationization Work Directive can be searched and viewed on the 'National Law Center'. Its evolution from 2011
to 2025 can be confirmed.

sections.title: 1. Environment A (Legacy KIATIS actual operation): from 2007 to at least 2022, KIATIS ran on
sections.body:
an internet‑facing server at DCIA and later at DIDC 1st Center in Yongin. Users at the 6·25 KIATIS
accessed the system over the public internet, from PCs and mobile devices, through browsers and
Active‑X controls, with no VPN and no database access control. The system was, in effect, a pseudo
web application that connected client machines directly to the database.

sections.title: 2. Environment D (New KIATIS actual operation after April 2021): only from 15 April 2021
sections.body:
onward did the new KIATIS begin to run in a more secure configuration, with a dedicated server in the
DIDC 1st Center’s defense network, Windows 10 clients without Active‑X, and the application of VPN
and database access control (SACRAMAX) for user access. Even then, the legacy KIATIS continued
to operate in parallel for some time.

sections.title: 3. Environment C (Integrated development and operational test environment, September 2019):
sections.body:
the September 2–11, 2019 development and operational test of new KIATIS was conducted in
DCIA’s internal network, not in the MKIA’s operational environment. The environment consisted of
PCs with Windows 10 and no Active‑X, connected via a special firewall rule from DCIA to the DIDC
database server. Crucially, no VPN or database access control was applied in this test environment;
the path from DCIA to the database was a direct, firewall‑mediated connection.

sections.title: 4. Environment B (hypothetical proper operational test environment): had the Directive been
sections.body:
followed and had the new KIATIS been tested in a properly separated operational test, the environment
would have been a controlled version of the MKIA’s own operational environment at the time—i.e., a
configuration closely matching how the system was actually used. In practice, that would have meant
an environment closely resembling A, unless and until the underlying architecture had been reengineered.
The key point is that Environment C, in which the test was actually conducted, did not
correspond either to A (the legacy reality) or to D (the eventual secure configuration), nor to B
(the proper operational test environment implied by the Directive). It was a bespoke, hybrid
environment that allowed the Cartel to produce a high test score (99.7 out of 100) while keeping the real
operational environment and its structural vulnerabilities out of view.

Comparing the operational environments and test evaluation environments of the new and old KIATIS
clearly reveals how irrational and absurd the military prosecution's fraud investigation has been. This
comparative analysis is a key piece of evidence presented in the author's "Explanation and Proof
Regarding Seizure, Search, and Warrant (September 2022, Record p. 4,979)" and is the most decisive
material directly exposing the Military Prosecutor's Office's fraudulent actions. As shown in <Figure 9>,
it compares the actual operating environments of the new and old KIATIS with the environment for
development and operational test and evaluation in the new KIATIS project.
First, the environment (Ⓐ) shown at the top and bottom left of Figure 6-1 represents the actual
environment where the old KIATIS was operated, at least until December 2022. The upper part of
Ⓐ represents the Defense Internet Homepage server, which was operated at the DCIA's Computer
Equipment Operation Center from 2007 to 2014 and subsequently moved to the Defense Integrated Data
Center 1 (Yongin). It indicates that the old KIATIS business programs were installed on that server and
operated over the internet. It was migrated to the integrated internet mail server completed on May
2019, and remained deployed and operational until at least December 2022.
The bottom of Ⓐ depicts an environment where MKIA personnel operated by directly accessing the
database for 15 years without security equipment like VPNs or DB access controls. They used remote
access via Galaxy Notes or PCs with Active-X installed. In other words, the environment shown on the
left side of Figure 6-1 (Ⓐ), representing the old KIATIS system, was the very "actual operational
environment" that was in use not only during the new KIATIS development and operational test
evaluation period from September 2 to September 2019, but also until December 2022.
Second, the environment (Ⓓ) shown in the middle top and bottom of Figure 6-1 depicts the actual
environment where the new KIATIS, applied only after April 2022, was operated. At this time, as
seen in the top portion of Ⓓ, it can be confirmed that servers used within the defense network operated
by the DIDC1 Center (Yongin) were utilized. Specifically, it shows the program completed through the
separate New KIATIS development project at the National Institute of Information and Communications
Technology (NICT) being deployed and operated on cloud-based servers established by the separate
New KIATIS server project at the DIDC1 Center (Yongin). VPN and DB access control (Sakra Max)
are also applied.
At the bottom of Ⓓ, the approximately 80 users of the new KIATIS utilize VPN and DB access control
via OTP cards on PCs running Windows 10 with Active-X removed. As seen at , this operates on a
different defense network from the old KIATIS environment (Ⓐ), existing in a distinct cyberspace. To
summarize, the new KIATIS environment (Ⓓ) shown in the middle of Figure 6-1 is the "actual
operational environment” that has been in operation since April 2022.
Finally, the environment (Ⓒ) shown at the top and bottom right of Figure 6-1 represents the new
KIATIS development, operation, and evaluation test conducted from September 2 to September

sections.title: 2019. Environment (Ⓒ) shares two similarities with Environment (Ⓓ). First, both environments are
sections.body:
Defense Network environments. Second, both environments feature Windows 10 operating systems
installed on PCs with Active-X removed.
On the other hand, they differ in two aspects. First, they have distinct intranet environments.
Environment (Ⓒ) is an artificially created environment within the DCIA's intranet for testing and
evaluation purposes, while Environment (Ⓓ) is the "actual operational environment" running within the
National Power Generation Corporation's intranet. Second, Environment (Ⓒ) is an environment where
VPN and DB access control (Sakra Max) are not applied, while Environment (Ⓓ) is an environment
where VPN and DB access control (Sakra Max) are applied. This difference stems from the time period
during which the environments operate. Both the National Institute of Standards and Technology's test
environment (Ⓒ) and the National Property Agency's operational environment (Ⓓ) lacked VPN and DB
access control (Sakra Max) until April 2021.
One point to note here is that at Layer the comparison between the actual operational environment
of the old and new KIATIS shown in Figure 4-8 and the test evaluation environment of the new KIATIS
project differs only in Environment (Ⓑ), while other environments are similar. Environment (Ⓑ)
represents the scenario where operational testing and evaluation were separated from the actual
operational environment at the MKIA, assuming a scenario where testing and evaluation were conducted
separately at the MKIA based on the Defense Informationization Work Directive.

. It must be

emphasized again that the test environment (Ⓒ) of the National Institute of Information and
Communications Technology (NICT) shown in Figure 6-1 at the sixth layer and the actual operational
environment (Ⓑ) of the National Agency for Public Administration and Security (NAPAS) shown in
Figure 4-8 at the fourth layer are identical in that they both operate without VPN and DB access control
(Sakra Max) applied. This is because VPN OTP cards were not available during the same time period
(September 2019, to April 2021).
Most importantly, what must not be overlooked here is that in the actual operational environment (Ⓑ)
of the National Property Agency, the PCs already used continuously within the existing PC and network
environment are on the "door keeper's allow list." Therefore, there is no need to request port opening
(duplicate allow list entries) from the new firewall (doorkeeper) to access the DB. Conversely, the
artificially created test environment (Ⓓ) at the DCIA for the new KIATIS development and operational
testing evaluation differs in several key aspects: it resides within the institute's intranet, meaning the
cyber assets (network, PCs, lines, data holdings, etc.) are distinct; consequently, new PCs must be
installed and assigned IP addresses for testing purposes; After that, to directly access the database on
the server at the DIDC 1 Center (the intended destination), requests must be made to add access
permission lists (port openings) to both the gatekeeper (firewall) at the National Defense Information
Agency and the gatekeeper (firewall) at the DIDC 1 Center. Considering remote work during the
COVID-19 period can make this easier to understand.
Thus, the procedure for using a new PC on the internet (intranet) is not only stipulated in the Defense
Informationization Directive but is also a globally agreed-upon standard regulation for information flow
between intranets in informatization environments.

sections.title: 3.4.3. Construction and Conduct of the Integrated Development and
sections.body:
Operational Test
Within this artificially constructed Environment C, the Cartel conducted what it called an “integrated
development and operational test.” The test was formally framed as verifying both development and
operational requirements, but, as the evidence shows, it was designed and executed in ways that served
the Cartel’s strategic aims rather than the project’s stated goals.
The test plan for the September 2019 exercise was drafted by a junior officer at DCIA and reported
directly to the DCIA director, bypassing the project management team leader (the author). The
evaluation committee included representatives from the MKIA and other stakeholders, but the author
was not a member. The test proceeded over ten days, during which users exercised the new KIATIS
functions in the DCIA environment, accessing the database through the firewall rule without VPN or
database access control.
At the end of the test, the evaluation committee awarded the system a score of 99.7 out of 100 and
declared it suitable for operational use (“operationally fit”). On the basis of this score, the new KIATIS
software development was formally accepted as successful. Yet, as Layer 1 has shown, the underlying
operational environment at the MKIA remained essentially unchanged: the legacy KIATIS continued to
operate over the internet without VPN or database access control, and the new KIATIS would not run
in the secure Environment D until well into 2021.
In parallel with the test, DCIA requested a firewall policy change at DIDC to allow the DCIA test
environment to access the KIATIS database. This request followed DIDC’s standard procedures for
firewall changes and was processed by DIDC’s security staff using established forms and workflows.

50 This was confirmed by the author of this paper upon inquiry to the DIDC section chief in
charge at the time in 2022 (July 2022, Record p. 3,271).

![Figure_10_Old_New_KIATIS_Operating_vs_Test_Environment.png](./images/Figure_10_Old_New_KIATIS_Operating_vs_Test_Environment.png)

Later, the Military Prosecutor’s Office would isolate this one request and treat it as a suspicious,
exceptional act, ignoring the broader context in which similar requests were routine for other systems
and in which the entire DIDC environment relied on such firewall rules for internal connectivity.
The integrated test thus served two functions. At the surface, it produced an impressive score that
could be used to claim success. At a deeper level, it created a documentary record—a “development and
operational test” report—that could later be invoked as proof that “operational testing” had been
conducted, even though the actual operational environment at the MKIA had never been scrutinized.

sections.title: 3.4.5. Logical and Mathematical Demonstration of Inconsistencies
sections.body:
The Korean treatise underpinning this paper includes a more formal analysis of the Cartel’s reasoning,
using tools from logic and truth‑table analysis. The essential point is that the Cartel’s claims about the
test environment and about the author’s alleged “manipulation” involve a series of identity and
temporal fallacies.
For example, prosecutorial documents state that the evaluation committee was required to test new
KIATIS “in an environment identical to the environment in which it would be used after fielding.” They
then treat the 2022 operational environment (Environment D, post‑April 2021, with VPN and DB access
control) as the relevant baseline and argue that the 2019 test environment (Environment C, without those
controls) was fraudulent because it did not match D.
Formally, this amounts to asserting that:
- the test environment in 2019 should have been identical to the operational environment in 2022.
- because it was not identical, those responsible for the test committed “obstruction of the performance
of official duties by fraudulent means.”
This is a time‑reversal fallacy. It demands that an event in 2019 conform to conditions that did not
exist until 2021 and then treats the absence of those future conditions as evidence of fraud. It also ignores
the fact that, as the internal KIATIS documents show, VPN and database access control were not applied
to KIATIS at all between August 2019 and April 2021.
Similarly, the prosecution conflates two distinct documents: (i) the evaluation committee’s final test
and evaluation report, which is the formal basis for accepting the development and declaring operational
suitability; and (ii) a later notification letter from DCIA to the Agency, which merely forwards the result.
Treating these as a single “decision” for the purpose of assigning responsibility is an identity fallacy; it
allows the blame for a collective committee decision to be shifted onto the signatory of a subsequent
informational letter.
In the Korean analysis, these and related assertions are represented in truth tables that show that the
Cartel’s reasoning leads to contradictions: under any consistent assignment of truth values to the
premises (about environments, times, and responsibilities), the conclusions cannot be sustained. This
formalization is not an academic exercise for its own sake; it serves to highlight that the Cartel’s legal
reasoning was not simply mistaken but structured around distortions of time, identity, and causality.

sections.title: 3.4.6. Time Reversal, Identity Fallacies, and the Reversal of Responsibility
sections.body:
The manipulations documented in this layer culminate in a broader inversion: the reversal of
responsibility between system and person, and between controller and subordinate.
By treating Environment D (the post‑2021 secure configuration) as if it had been the relevant
operational environment in 2019, the Cartel was able to recast the normal, documented configuration of
the KIATIS test environment (Environment C) as a deviation. By conflating distinct documents and
roles, it could portray an officer who had never been a member of the evaluation committee and who
had not approved the test plan as the “manipulator” of the test environment.
Meanwhile, those who had designed, approved, and operated the legacy KIATIS configuration
(Environment A), who had overseen its migration to the integrated mail server, who had decided not to
apply VPN and database access control to KIATIS until 2021, and who had orchestrated the integrated
test and KIDA study, remained offstage. Their actions were either buried under layers of organizational
role‑shifting or framed as routine technical and regulatory work.
The background and motive of manipulation and intentional exclusion of DIDC, even violating

The meeting of 8 February 2022, held at the MKIA and attended by DCIA, DIDC, MKIA staff, and
the maintenance contractor’s project manager, was ostensibly convened to discuss “improvement
directions” for the new KIATIS operation. In practice, it became the trigger point for the Cartel’s open
move against the author.
During the meeting, the MKIA’s excavation team leader spoke candidly. He explained that, in practice,
his team members carried Galaxy Note smartphones and used them to access KIATIS during field
operations, that the system had been used for years without VPN, and that the recent introduction of
VPN cards for new KIATIS had made their work more cumbersome. He described VPN as a “strange”
and unnecessary burden, suggesting that from the team’s perspective, the legacy arrangement had been
more convenient.
The author responded by pointing out that the fundamental issue was not the inconvenience of VPN
but the structural error of having operated a defense database over the public internet for fifteen years
without VPN or database access control. He noted that the proper solution was not to remove VPN but
to consider a different architecture, such as a web‑based message‑queue approach that would satisfy
both operational needs and security requirements. In doing so, he explicitly connected the daily
experience of the excavation teams with the systemic violations he had documented.
Within forty‑eight hours of this meeting, on 10 February 2022, a junior officer at DCIA filed an “abuse
of power” complaint against the author with the DCIA director and the Investigation Headquarters. The
complaint did not allege that he had threatened or assaulted anyone at the 8 February meeting. Instead,
it focused on alleged “abusive” behavior in his management of the junior officer and others over
preceding months. On the basis of this complaint, the director immediately ordered the author removed
from his office and placed in a single‑person room, where he remained for more than six months.
The speed and character of this response are not compatible with the hypothesis of a purely
interpersonal conflict. They are, however, fully consistent with a pre‑planned strategy: once the
author’s understanding of KIATIS’s structural vulnerabilities and their connection to the 2016 hacking
incident became visible, the Cartel moved to isolate him physically, stigmatize him socially, and prepare
the ground for a legal attack.

sections.title: 3.5.2. Anatomy of the False Abuse Complaint and the Mechanics of Solitary
sections.body:
Confinement
The abuse complaint itself, when read alongside subsequent statements and records, displays all the
hallmarks of a constructed narrative. It alleges that the author routinely humiliated and overworked
subordinates, that he used harsh language, and that he created a “fearful atmosphere” in the office. Many
of these allegations are couched in vague terms, lack concrete dates or witnesses, and contradict earlier
positive performance evaluations and expressions of trust by superiors.
The Investigation Headquarters and the Defense Audit bodies treated the complaint as a trigger for an
“abuse investigation.” The first step was immediate removal and isolation. On the day the complaint
was received, the author was ordered to vacate his office, forbidden from accessing his usual
workstations and document repositories, and assigned to a single‑person room with minimal contact with
others. Officially, this was justified as a measure to “protect subordinates.” In realities it served to cut
him off from information, from evidence, and from informal support.
During this period, the author was effectively prevented from participating in daily work, from
attending meetings, and from seeing how the KIATIS project and related systems were being discussed
and managed. His only formal interactions with the organization occurred in the context of the abuse
investigation and, later, the Military Prosecutor’s investigation. The asymmetry of information was
complete: those who were coordinating the Cartel’s response had access to all relevant documents,
systems, and personnel; the author had access only to such materials as he had previously collected or
could request through formal channels that were now controlled by his adversaries.
Meanwhile, <Table 3-5-1> shows documents exchanged between the National Intelligence Service,
the Ministry of National Defense Prosecutor's Office, and the Investigation Headquarters regarding the
hacking incident and the new KIATIS system investigation from 2021 to 2022. It reveals intensive
communication while the whistleblower was confined to a solitary cell.

sections.title: 3.5.3. The Fraudulent Audit: Scripted Testimony and Pre‑Determined Conclusions
sections.body:
Parallel to the author’s isolation, the Defense Audit bodies and the Investigation Headquarters
conducted what they described as an “abuse audit.” They interviewed the complainant, other
subordinates, and colleagues, producing transcripts and summaries that became part of the official record.
A close reading of these materials reveals a pattern of leading questions, selective recording, and
one‑sided interpretation.
In several interviews, auditors introduced specific phrases or interpretations and then recorded the
interviewee’s agreement as evidence. For example, they would ask whether the author’s insistence on
adhering to regulations made subordinates feel “oppressed,” or whether his criticisms of project designs
were “humiliating.” When interviewees expressed mixed or nuanced views—acknowledging both the
author’s strictness and his professionalism—only the negative aspects were highlighted in the summaries.
Positive statements about the author’s competence, fairness, or dedication were often omitted or
downplayed. In some cases, witnesses later reported that their words had been taken out of context or
that they did not recognize themselves in the official write‑ups. The overall structure of the audit
materials reflects not an open inquiry but an exercise in building a dossier that would support a
conclusion reached in advance: that the author was an abusive manager whose removal was justified.
The audit report’s findings and recommendations mirrored this bias. They accepted the complainant’s
narrative at face value, ignored contrary evidence, and extended their conclusions to issues far beyond
the specific allegations, suggesting that the author’s mere presence in a leadership role was problematic.
At no point did the audit examine the context of his work: the unusual concentration of responsibilities
he bore, the chronic underfunding and mismanagement of key projects, or the likelihood that his
insistence on standards had created tension with those seeking to avoid scrutiny.
Thus, the audit functioned as a fraudulent legitimating device. It gave institutional form to the stigma
that the Cartel wished to attach to the author and provided a pseudo‑legal basis for keeping him isolated
and discredited, thereby smoothing the way for the Military Prosecutor’s later intervention.

sections.title: 3.5.4. “Witness Elimination Operation”: Time, Structure, and Intent
sections.body:
When viewed in isolation, each element of Layer 5—the meeting of 8 February, the complaint of 10
February, the isolation, the audit interviews, the stigmatizing narratives—might be explained away as an
unfortunate but ordinary organizational conflict. When integrated into the seven‑layer proof system, their
timing, structure, and content take on a different meaning.
First, the timing is too precise to be coincidental. The author had been working on the KIATIS case
and on related issues for years. Yet it was only when the legacy system’s internet operation and VPN‑less
configuration were openly acknowledged in a multi‑party meeting, and when the author explicitly linked
this fact to structural security violations, that the abuse complaint materialized. The forty‑eight‑hour
interval between his statement and the complaint is consistent with a pre‑positioned retaliation plan
awaiting a trigger.
Second, the structure of the response mirrors that of an APT‑style operation. The initial
reconnaissance phase had already been completed: the Cartel knew the author’s personality, his working
style, his vulnerabilities, and his political isolation as a strict, principled officer. The complaint and audit
constituted the first overt exploitation. Isolation and reputational attacks represented lateral movement
and privilege escalation: once the author was socially and institutionally weakened, more severe
measures could be applied with less resistance.
Third, the intent becomes clear when Layer 6 is taken into account. The abuse complaint and
fraudulent audit were not ends in themselves. They prepared the terrain for the Military Prosecutor’s
targeted investigation. When the Prosecutor’s Office later framed the author as a manipulator of the
KIATIS test environment and as an obstructionist, it could rely on the pre‑existing stigma of “abuse” and
on the aura of official audits to support its narrative. The “witness” had been transformed, in the eyes of
many, into someone whose credibility could be discounted in advance.
From this perspective, the events of Layer 5 amount to a witness elimination operation in the broad
sense. The author was not physically eliminated, but his ability to speak, to be heard, and to function as
an effective witness was systematically destroyed. The operation exploited the full repertoire of
organizational tools—complaints, audits, isolation, rumor, and character assassination—under the guise
of normal procedure.

sections.title: 3.5.5. Documentary and Testimonial Evidence Underpinning Layer 5
sections.body:
The conclusions in this layer rest on a rich body of primary materials. These include:
– the Transcript and recording from the 8 February 2022 “new KIATIS improvement” meeting,
including the excavation team leader’s statements about long‑term VPN‑less operation.
– the abuse complaint filed on 10 February 2022, with its timing, content, and lack of specific
corroboration.
– the orders removing the author from his office and assigning him to a single‑person room.
– the transcripts and summaries of audit interviews, including instances of leading questions, selective
recording, and discrepancies between witnesses’ recollections and the official record;
– internal messages and informal communications that show how stigmatizing narratives about the
author were spread within DCIA and related organizations.
– the author’s own contemporaneous notes and subsequent reconstructions in documents such as his
detailed rebuttal to the abuse allegations and his “abuse investigation exoneration” analysis.
Taken together, these materials leave little doubt that the abuse complaint and subsequent audit were
means, not ends. They were instrumental steps in a larger operation aimed at removing the author from
his position, discrediting his testimony, and preparing the way for a prosecutorial narrative that would
shield the Cartel and isolate him as an alleged wrongdoer.

sections.title: 3.5.6. Closing the Fifth Layer
sections.body:
Layer 5 reveals a crucial intermediate mechanism in the Defense Informatization Cartel’s scheme.
Having engineered the legacy KIATIS configuration (Layer 1), manipulated the governance of the new
KIATIS project (Layer 2), and excluded the author from control over the test (Layer 3), and having
orchestrated a manipulated integrated test and regulatory cover (Layer 4), the Cartel now turned to the
problem posed by the author as a knowledgeable, resistant insider.
The solution it selected was not to confront his arguments, but to attack his person: to redefine him
as an abuser, to isolate him physically and socially, to orchestrate a sham audit that would produce the
desired label, and to carry that label forward into the legal arena. In doing so, the Cartel successfully
degraded his ability to act as a witness and created a context in which his later prosecution would seem,
to outsiders, like an extension of a justified disciplinary response rather than the culmination of an
APT‑style human targeting operation.
With this layer, the bridge between the technical and legal manipulations on the one hand and the
human consequence on the other becomes fully visible. The next layer, Layer analyzes how the
Military Prosecutor’s Office took over from the auditors and converted the Cartel’s organizational
campaign into a formal “fraud investigation,” completing the transformation of a whistleblower into a
purported criminal.

Sixth Layer: The Military Prosecutor’s Blind Fraud Investigation

sections.title: 3.6.1. Overview of Layer 6
sections.body:
Layer 6 is the final operational arena in which the Defense Informatization Cartel’s long‑prepared
strategy is executed through the military criminal justice system. Whereas Layers 1 to 5 document how
the Cartel manipulated systems, regulations, tests, evaluations, and internal audits to conceal the true
causes of the 2016 DIDC hacking incident, Layer 6 shows how the Military Prosecutor’s Office
converted those manipulations into a fraudulent criminal investigation that branded the author a
“criminal” in order to protect the organization. In this sense, Layer 6 is the “end state” of the entire
scheme: the point at which normative legal procedures are outwardly preserved, but their substantive
function is inverted.
The central claim of Layer 6 is that the anti‑corruption investigation conducted by the Military
Prosecutor’s Office was not an attempt to identify wrongdoing, but a state‑orchestrated fraud
investigation designed to (i) shield the Cartel’s long‑term violations of the Defense Informatization
Directive and national law and (ii) secure a formal decision that would permanently discredit the main
witness. The investigation process—from the issuance and execution of search and seizure warrants,
through the framing of charges and interrogation questions, to the final non‑prosecution decision—
systematically ignored or suppressed exculpatory evidence, misrepresented the technical and
organizational realities documented in earlier layers, and misdirected responsibility away from those
who actually designed and approved the relevant systems and policies.
This layer relies on the evidentiary foundation established in Layers 1 through It presupposes that
the legacy KIATIS ran for fifteen years on the public internet without VPN or database access control;
that the new KIATIS project was governed in violation of the Defense Informatization Directive; that
the integrated test and evaluation were structurally manipulated and later academicized through KIDA’s
research; and that the false “abuse of power” complaint and sham audit functioned as a precursor to
criminalization. On that basis, Layer 6 analyzes how the Military Prosecutor’s Office took a set of
previously normalized, formally approved actions and retroactively reclassified them as criminal acts,
while simultaneously leaving untouched the long‑term systemic violations that created the security
breach in the first place.

sections.title: 3.6.2. Elements of Crime and Their Relationship to the Fraud Investigation
sections.body:
To show that the investigation itself constitutes a form of crime, the analysis in this layer begins from
the classical structure of criminal liability: statutory elements (actus reus and mens rea),
unlawfulness, and responsibility. In conventional criminal law doctrine, an offense is established only
when the conduct satisfies the legal definition of a specific crime, is not justified, and can be attributed
to the actor as blameworthy. Cybercrime doctrine adds to this structure the specific features of the digital
environment, such as non‑physical intrusion, anonymity, broad reach, and speed.
When the Military Prosecutor’s conduct is examined through this lens, a striking inversion appears.
The prosecutors treated the author’s past work as if it satisfied all the elements of an offense, while their
own acts of manipulation—selective use of documents, suppression of exculpatory records, and
misrepresentation of system configurations—closely resemble the elements of fraud. In terms of
deceptive conduct, they constructed a narrative according to which a 99.7‑percent‑successful test of the
new KIATIS became a “failed test,” and the absence of VPN and database access control functions
during the relevant period was recast as if these controls had existed and had been improperly “hidden”
from the evaluation committee. In terms of intent, they acted with the clear purpose of protecting the
organization, shifting attention away from fifteen years of structural non‑compliance and toward an
individual officer.
The investigation also exhibits the typical features of cyber‑enabled crime. It was conducted largely
through documents and digital traces, without direct confrontation or transparent adversarial process. It
relied on the non‑face‑to‑face character of bureaucratic communication: warrants were issued,
documents were seized and interpreted, and decisions were drafted in offices far removed from the
original operational context, while the target was isolated and deprived of effective means to respond.
The anonymizing effect of institutional hierarchy enabled responsibility to be diffused across
investigators, supervising prosecutors, and institutional superiors. In this way, the investigation
transformed the conceptual apparatus of criminal law into an instrument for the abuse of state power.

sections.title: 3.6.3. Proof of the Fraud Investigation
sections.body:
Layer 6 demonstrates the fraudulent character of the investigation through a careful reconstruction of
the sequence of investigative acts and the associated records. The first point of analysis is the search
and seizure process. Warrants that should have been targeted at identifying the actual operational
environment of KIATIS—whether VPN and database access control existed during the test period, how
firewall policies were configured, who requested and approved specific changes—were instead used to
harvest documents that could be selectively interpreted against the author. At the same time, key
documents that clearly showed the non‑existence of VPN and access control during the relevant period
were either ignored in the investigative reasoning or treated as peripheral.
The interrogation phase further reveals the pre‑structured nature of the investigation. Questions were
framed on the assumption that the test environment for the new KIATIS was equivalent to the actual
operational environment, and that any discrepancy between the two would automatically constitute
unlawful concealment. This assumption contradicted both the Defense Informatization Directive as it
stood at the time and the documented practice that test environments ordinarily differ from production
environments. Moreover, the prosecutors’ questions presupposed that the author had been in a position
to participate in, influence, or control the test and evaluation process, despite organizational charts and
duty assignments showing that he was not a member of the evaluation committee and had no authority
over the final test design.
By juxtaposing the interrogation records with the organizational documents and technical logs
established in earlier layers, the analysis shows that the investigation was not an open‑ended search
for truth, but a process aimed at extracting statements that could later be cited as admissions. The
author’s answers, however, consistently aligned with the documentary record: he explained that VPN
and database access controls did not exist during the relevant test period, that he did not attend or
supervise the evaluation sessions, and that the discrepancies between the test and operational
environments were designed and approved by others. In the long run, these very records have become a
linchpin that supports the conclusion that the investigation was fraudulent.

sections.title: 3.6.4. The “Non‑Prosecution Statement” as a Model of Institutionalized
sections.body:
Deception
One of the most revealing artifacts in Layer 6 is the non‑prosecution statement (or equivalent
decision document) issued by the Military Prosecutor’s Office. On its face, the document appears to be
an act of leniency: the case is closed without indictment, ostensibly in the subject’s favor. Yet a close
reading shows that it serves a very different function. It implicitly affirms the investigative narrative that
the author’s conduct was problematic, while stopping short of subjecting the case to judicial scrutiny.
In this sense, the non‑prosecution decision operates as a model of institutionalized deception. It
allows the Cartel to claim that the matter was “duly investigated” and “resolved,” while avoiding a court
process that might expose the inconsistencies of the investigation and the underlying structural
violations in Layers 1 to The decision is drafted in a way that preserves the appearance of evaluative
judgment—referring

control during the test period, the author’s lack of evaluative authority, and the responsibility of

Seen in the broader context of the seven‑layer system, the non‑prosecution decision completes a circle.
The same manipulated test results and doctored regulatory interpretations that were created in Layer
and the same social and reputational pressures generated in Layer are recycled here as background

stigmatized as a person who has been investigated for serious wrongdoing and spared only by
prosecutorial grace.

sections.title: 3.6.5. Why the New KIATIS Project Was Really Delayed
sections.body:
Layer 6 also clarifies a causal question that is central to the Cartel’s narrative: why the new KIATIS
performance improvement project was delayed and why it failed to reach timely full deployment.
The Cartel’s version of events implies that the delay resulted from technical or managerial shortcomings
attributable to the author and his work as project manager. The evidence assembled across the seven
layers tells a different story.

The delay was structurally overdetermined by the legacy of the old KIATIS and by the Cartel’s
choices. The decision to run the legacy system for fifteen years without proper security controls created

Rather than confronting this contradiction, the Cartel chose to manipulate the test design, to integrate
development and operational evaluation in ways that obscured the real operational risks, and to use

In this context, the fraud investigation against the author served a tactical purpose. It allowed the
Cartel to redefine the cause of delay as an individual’s supposed failure to disclose technical details to
the evaluation committee, instead of acknowledging systemic misgovernance and long‑term regulatory
non‑compliance. The disparity between this narrative and the documented sequence of decisions—who

sections.title: 3.7. Seventh Layer: Petitions, Calls for Investigation, and the Final
sections.body:
Non‑Prosecution Decision

Layer 7 examines what happened after the internal mechanisms of the Defense Informatization Cartel
had effectively completed their work. At this stage, the author, now stigmatized as a suspect but still
convinced that the system could correct itself from within, turned to the highest authorities in the military
and to external oversight bodies. He submitted detailed petitions, supporting materials, and explanatory
memoranda to the Minister of National Defense, the Minister’s military advisor, the head and
human‑rights supervisor of the Military Prosecutor’s Office, and later to the Anti‑Corruption and Civil
Rights Commission and the National Human Rights Commission. 54
The central finding of this layer is that these efforts did not lead to substantive review or correction.
Instead, the authorities either remained silent, engaged in formalistic processing of the submissions, or,
in some cases, sought to channel the materials back to the very institutions whose conduct was in
question. The final outcome was a non‑prosecution decision that left the Cartel’s structure intact,
preserved the stigmatizing effect of the investigation on the author, and reinforced the overall closure of
the case.

sections.title: 3.7.1. Petitions and Calls for Investigation Inside the Military
sections.body:
The first set of actions in Layer 7 took place within the military hierarchy itself. The author addressed
the Minister of National Defense directly, sending a comprehensive petition that included his detailed
analysis of the search and seizure warrant, the contradictions in the Military Prosecutor’s reasoning, and
the structural violations of the Defense Informatization Directive. The petition was transmitted via the
intranet e‑mail system and accompanied by a memorandum report; confirmation records indicated that
it had been opened. The same materials were forwarded to the Minister’s military advisor via secure
messaging.
In parallel, the author submitted petitions to the Military Prosecutor’s Office, including to the
human‑rights supervisor. In these petitions he did not merely protest his own treatment. He described,
in institutional terms, how the Pentagon‑style logic of the Cartel operated: how the same actors who had

along with a request to the Chief of the Military Prosecutors' Office for a no-suspicion disposition and for the

the human-rights supervisor, my own criminal-record report, the project’s related agencies, and via the On-Nara

designed and approved the long‑term non‑compliant operation of legacy KIATIS and the manipulated

diverted attention to a single officer. During interrogations, prosecutors and investigators explicitly
acknowledged key facts, such as the fifteen‑year VPN‑free operation of the legacy system, yet these
acknowledgments did not translate into corrective action.
The author also addressed the head of the Military Prosecutor’s Office, urging a swift non‑guilty
disposition. In a subsequent conversation, the gap between the formal language of the institution and the
substance of the case became clear: the leadership acknowledged the exceptional complexity of the
matter but did not commit to revisiting the investigative frame itself. The petitions thus revealed a
