sections.title: 3.7.2. Petitions to Civilian Oversight Bodies and Their Responses
sections.body:
After the non‑prosecution decision, the author turned to civilian oversight institutions: the
Anti‑Corruption and Civil Rights Commission and the National Human Rights Commission. He
submitted thousands of pages of evidence, including contract documents, directive histories, official
correspondence, interrogation records, and his own analytical memoranda. The expectation was that
these bodies, formally independent and tasked with protecting citizens against state abuse, would at
minimum conduct interviews, review the documentary record, and issue reasoned findings.
The responses, however, were characterized by delay, evasion, and a tendency to treat the case as an
administrative burden rather than a substantive challenge. In one instance, an official at the
Anti‑Corruption Commission openly discussed the possibility of transmitting the author’s materials
back to the Ministry of National Defense, effectively returning the evidence of corruption to the
institution alleged to be corrupt. No effort was made to hear the author as a witness, despite the scale
and coherence of the materials submitted.
The National Human Rights Commission behaved in a similar manner. The petitions were formally
received, but internal transfers, procedural excuses, and eventual summary dispositions meant that the
substance of the claims—prolonged solitary confinement, reputational destruction, and organized
targeting for whistleblowing—was never examined in depth. Final notices were framed in generic
bureaucratic language that invoked competence limits and procedural constraints, without engaging with
the core evidence. In this way, the oversight bodies functioned less as independent guardians and more
as buffers that absorbed and dispersed the pressure generated by the author’s claims.

sections.title: 3.7.3. The Author as Subject and Witness
sections.body:
Layer 7 also brings into focus the position of the author as both the subject of the operation and the
primary witness to it. He is a retired lieutenant colonel with nearly thirty‑two years of service in the
Ministry of National Defense and the Army, specializing in information technology development and
acquisition. Over the course of his career, he worked at the intersection of operational units, IT
development organizations, and defense policy, acquiring a rare combination of technical,
organizational, and legal literacy.
This background is crucial to understanding why he was targeted and how he was able to reconstruct
the seven‑layer system. His professional experience enabled him to recognize that the legacy KIATIS
configuration violated basic security principles and legal requirements, to understand the significance
of the test‑environment manipulations for the new system, and to decipher the meaning of directive
amendments and internal memoranda that would otherwise appear innocuous. At the same time, his
insistence on applying these standards consistently—to himself, to his colleagues, and to the
institution—made him an obstacle to the Cartel’s strategy of denial and concealment.
Layer 7 thus completes the evidentiary structure of the paper. It shows that even when comprehensive
petitions and detailed evidence are presented to the highest military and civilian authorities, structural
incentives and institutional self‑protection can prevent effective redress. The non‑prosecution decision
and the dismissal or deflection of petitions do not refute the seven‑layer proof system; they confirm its
central insight: in this case, state power did not fail to act because it was ignorant of the facts, but because
the facts, once acknowledged, would have required the state to indict itself.

sections.title: 4. Comparison with Hannah Arendt’s Concept of the
sections.body:
“Banality of Evil”
4.1.

The Concept and Features of the “Banality of Evil”

sections.title: 4.1.1. The Eichmann Case in Eichmann in Jerusalem
sections.body:
In Eichmann in Jerusalem, Hannah Arendt introduced the notion of the “banality of evil”, a concept
that fundamentally reshaped the study of modern, large‑scale crime. Her central observation was that
Adolf Eichmann, a key organizer of the Holocaust logistics, was not a demonic monster in appearance
or self‑conception, but a thoroughly ordinary bureaucrat who had stopped thinking. He repeatedly
insisted that he had merely “followed orders” and obeyed the law of the state, and he denied possessing
any particularly intense personal hatred of Jews.
For Arendt, the most disturbing aspect of Eichmann was not monstrous passion, but thoughtlessness.
He claimed to have followed Kant’s categorical imperative, yet in practice he internalized a perverted
maxim—“the Führer’s will is the supreme law”—and allowed that maxim to replace his own moral
judgment. Moral inversion occurred when obedience to authority was redefined as virtue, and
independent reflection on the human consequences of one’s actions was suspended. In this sense, the
“banality” in Arendt’s phrase refers not to the triviality of the crimes, but to the ordinary, routinized
character of the people and practices that made them possible.

sections.title: 4.1.2. General Patterns of Organizational Crime
sections.body:
Arendt’s analysis highlights several structural patterns that recur in organizational crime. The first is
bureaucratic division of labor. Complex tasks are broken down into narrow roles, and each individual
is responsible only for a small segment of the overall process. No single person sees the full picture, and
thus each can say that he or she merely performed a specific duty. The second is a chain of command
that privileges obedience over judgment. Within such a structure, “I only followed orders” comes to
be perceived as a sufficient moral defense, and personal responsibility is displaced upwards to distant
superiors who themselves may invoke systemic necessity.
The third pattern is the construction of an appearance of normalcy. Actions that would be morally
shocking if described in plain language—such as deportation, expropriation, or targeted destruction of
livelihoods—are rephrased as “administrative measures,” “procedural adjustments,” or “routine
enforcement.” This linguistic and procedural normalization dulls moral sensibility and makes it possible
for ordinary people to participate in extraordinary wrongdoing without experiencing themselves as
criminals.
In Arendt’s framework, evil takes on a bureaucratic form. It is not primarily driven by sadistic pleasure
or explicit ideological hatred at the level of every participant; rather, it emerges from a system that
rewards conformity, punishes dissent, and translates morally loaded decisions into neutral‑sounding
administrative acts.

sections.title: 4.2. Specificity and Atrocity of the Present Case
4.2.1. Nearly Four Years of Continuous Targeting of a Single Military Officer
sections.body:
When viewed from a socio‑political perspective, the APT‑style human targeting documented in this
treatise shares some structural features with the patterns Arendt identified, but it also exhibits a
qualitatively different intensity and direction. In this case, the focal point is not a large, anonymous
population, but a single military officer with thirty‑two years of service, targeted continuously over
approximately three years and eleven months, from 3 December 2018 to 31 October 2022.
The targeting began when the officer was reassigned to a new position in a manner that, in retrospect,
appears calculated rather than accidental. Over the subsequent years, he was subjected to a layered
sequence of measures: professional isolation, manipulation of duty assignments, a false “abuse of
power” complaint, solitary confinement in the workplace, organized reputational attacks, a fraudulent
criminal investigation, and ultimately a forced dishonorable retirement. These measures were not
random. They corresponded closely to the Defense Informatization Cartel’s evolving need to neutralize
a witness who had uncovered the true root cause of the 2016 DIDC hacking incident.
From an analytical standpoint, this pattern can be described as organizational stalking. Unlike the
mass violence of Eichmann’s context, the present case involves a many‑to‑one structure in which
multiple institutions track, contain, and attempt to destroy the social and professional existence of a
single individual. The cruelty lies not only in the severity of the outcome, but in the precision with which
the target was selected and studied. The author’s personality traits (intolerance of injustice, intellectual
independence), his rare combination of technical and legal expertise, his career trajectory as an IT officer
and one of the few officers with his particular background—all of these were effectively exploited to
design a tailored campaign of pressure and marginalization.

sections.title: 4.2.2. Systematic Manipulation Across Seven Layers
sections.body:
The seven‑layer proof system reveals a degree of structural integration that, in some respects, exceeds
the bureaucratic efficiency observed by Arendt. Each layer—ranging from the long‑term operation of
the legacy KIATIS, through the governance of the new system and the manipulation of tests, to the abuse
complaint, the sham audits, the fraudulent investigation, and the handling of petitions—plays a distinct
role in a single, coherent scheme.
Particularly striking is the combination of technical, legal, and academic manipulation. The
Defense Informatization Cartel did not merely violate existing rules; it rewrote them. It used an
“Active‑X removal” project to erase incriminating traces of legacy KIATIS, engineered changes to the
Defense Informatization Directive to retroactively legitimize irregular practices, and relied on KIDA’s
evaluation improvement research to provide an ostensibly scholarly rationale for an integrated
development‑and‑operational test design that obscured the real operational environment.
This is not simply an instance of thoughtless obedience. It is an example of what might be called the
intellectualization of evil. The actors anticipated future scrutiny and constructed a layered system of
plausible deniability, in which each document, directive amendment, and research report could be
invoked as evidence that they had acted “according to procedure.” In this sense, the case moves beyond
the “banality of evil” toward a state in which organizational actors engage in sophisticated planning, not
only to commit wrongdoing but to pre‑engineer the evidentiary environment in which that
wrongdoing will later be judged.

sections.title: 4.2.3. Organizational Crime Aimed at the Total Destruction of an Individual
sections.body:
The evidentiary record also shows that the ultimate objective of the operation was not merely to
silence dissent, but to destroy the targeted individual as a social and professional person. The
mechanisms of destruction were multi‑dimensional:
(1) Social reputation was attacked through the false abuse complaint, the circulation of negative
narratives within the organization, and the framing of the author as a problematic superior.
(2) Legal status was undermined through the fraud investigation and the formal classification of the
author as a suspect, culminating in a non‑prosecution decision that left a lasting stigma.
(3) Economic and professional prospects were damaged by forcing an early, dishonorable retirement,
thereby cutting off future opportunities that would normally follow from a thirty‑two‑year military
career.
This configuration resembles, in a contemporary institutional setting, the creation of what George
Orwell called an “unperson”: someone whose public standing, credibility, and institutional identity are
systematically erased. Whereas the Nazi crimes that Arendt analyzed sought to negate the existence of
Jews as a group, the present case can be understood as an attempt to negate the existence of a
truth‑telling individual whose persistence threatened to expose a long‑concealed structural failure.
From the standpoint of democratic governance, this is not only a crime against a person, but a crime
against the possibility of institutional self‑correction. It signals to potential whistleblowers that those
who uncover inconvenient truths may be punished not despite their integrity, but because of it.

sections.title: 4.3. Evolution and New Patterns of Organizational Crime
4.3.1. Targeting Innocent Individuals Through Reputational Terror and Legal
sections.body:
Neutralization
The case examined in this treatise illustrates how organizational crime has evolved in the digital age.
Cyber‑APT concepts—persistence, precision, stealth—have migrated from the technical realm into the
social and legal domains. The same institutions that learned to defend against external threats have
adapted APT‑style tactics to target internal actors who threaten to reveal institutional vulnerabilities.
The process begins with target selection and framing. An individual who possesses critical
knowledge and moral resolve becomes a threat vector. He is then framed as a management problem, a
source of “abuse,” or a disruptive presence. Once this frame is in place, a series of administrative and
legal steps can be justified: removal from key positions, initiation of internal complaints, isolation under
the pretext of investigation, and the construction of a paper trail that presents the victim as the cause of
organizational instability.
This is accompanied by reputational terror. Whisper campaigns, selective disclosure of partial
information, and the strategic leakage of negative characterizations contribute to an environment in
which colleagues distance themselves from the target, either out of genuine belief or self‑protection.
When legal procedures—search warrants, interrogations, non‑prosecution decisions—are then layered
on top of this reputational erosion, the result is a form of legal neutralization. Even without a formal
conviction, the person is transformed into someone whose testimony can be dismissed as biased,
unreliable, or self‑serving.
The seven‑layer system documented in this paper shows that such operations are not accidental or
purely emotional reactions. They are structurally reproducible patterns that exploit the interfaces
between technical systems, legal frameworks, organizational hierarchies, and social psychology. If
Arendt diagnosed the twentieth century with the “banality of evil,” this case suggests that
twenty‑first‑century institutions may require us to confront a new configuration: the strategic,
data‑driven, and legally coded deployment of evil against those who attempt to speak the truth
from within the system.

sections.title: 5. Conclusion and Implications
sections.body:
The lack of criminal liability for the author of this paper and the petitioner is clearly proven through
technical, legal, and organizational analysis. This case is not an individual crime but an organized,
collective, and systematic crime committed by the <Defense Information Cartel Group>, centered on
the Military Prosecutor's Office and its prosecutors, investigators, public officials, military personnel,
Defense Research Institute members, and sympathizers, in connection with the 2016 North Korean
hacking incident. This is a power-based crime committed by the Ministry of National Defense's power
institutions. At the core of the <Defense Informationization Cartel Group>'s crimes lies the systematic
concealment, collusion, manipulation, and heinous human rights violations aimed at covering up the
fact that they continuously operated the same security vulnerabilities for 15 years, violating major
national statutes and Ministry of National Defense directives, identical to those exploited in the 2016
DIDC hacking incident.
To achieve this, the Ministry of National Defense's military prosecution and the defense cartel illegally
abused their power and authority over an extended period. They distorted directives and manipulated
guidelines by reporting plausible reasons in documents such as the Director-General's Information
Technology Operations Directive and operational manuals under plausible pretexts. They then distorted
the directives and manipulated the manuals. They moved military personnel who had served honorably
and justly for 32 years like pawns on a chessboard, transferring them without their knowledge or consent.
This served as a means to conceal crimes and shift responsibility, timed to suit their own operations.
Strengthening transparency within the defense information organization, preventing cartels, and
establishing a human rights protection system are urgent. Prompt exoneration, compensation for victims,
personal safety guarantees for this petitioner and their family, and punishment of the perpetrators are
necessary.

sections.title: 5.1. Results of the Evidence‑Based, Layered Proof
5.1.1. Establishing Organized Crime Through a Seven‑Layer Proof System
sections.body:
This paper has argued, and the underlying treatise has demonstrated, that the author is innocent in
criminal, technical, and organizational terms, and that the real wrongdoing lies in the long‑term,
coordinated acts of a Defense Informatization Cartel centered in the Ministry of National Defense and
its affiliated organizations. The seven‑layer proof system does not rest on conjecture or selective
anecdotes. It is built on the close analysis of approximately 12,500 pages of primary and secondary
materials, including national contract law provisions, Defense Informatization Directives, project plans
and requests for proposals, official correspondence on the On‑Nara system, KIDA research outputs,
interrogation records, and petitions to internal and external authorities.
Layer 1 established that the legacy KIATIS system, which handled sensitive 6·25 war casualty
information, was operated for about fifteen years directly on the public internet, without VPN or
database access control, in violation of basic security principles and the Defense Informatization
Directive. Layer 2 showed how the new KIATIS performance improvement project was structured and
documented in a way that redistributed formal responsibility, misclassified the project, and laid the
groundwork for future blame‑shifting. Layer 3 clarified that, during his tenure at DCIA, the author
performed his duties as a project manager normally and diligently, without any role in the design or
execution of the later test and evaluation that became the supposed basis of criminal suspicion.
Layer 4 exposed the core manipulation of the test and evaluation environment, KIDA’s role in
producing an “evaluation improvement” study that normalized this manipulation, and the engineering
of regulatory amendments that retroactively legitimated irregular practices. Layer 5 demonstrated that
the false “abuse of power” complaint and the subsequent sham audit were not isolated personal conflicts
but deliberate components of a witness elimination strategy. Layer 6 reconstructed the military
prosecutor’s investigation as a form of fraud, in which exculpatory technical facts were suppressed or
inverted, and a non‑prosecution decision was used to close the case while preserving the stigma. Finally,
Layer 7 documented the failure of internal and external oversight mechanisms to correct these wrongs,
despite detailed petitions and extensive evidence.
Taken together, the seven layers depict a single, integrated system of organized crime. Each layer
contributes a necessary function: erasing technical history, redistributing formal responsibility,
manipulating tests and regulations, neutralizing an internal critic, and closing off avenues of redress.
The result is a coherent pattern of conduct that satisfies the substantive elements of organizational crime:
planned concealment of a serious security failure, systematic abuse of institutional power, and deliberate
infliction of harm on an individual who discovered and disclosed the truth.

sections.title: 5.1.2. The Contribution of a Discovery‑Oriented, Language‑Analytic Method
sections.body:
A distinctive feature of this work is methodological. The analysis extends J. L. Austin’s
discovery‑oriented approach to ordinary language into the domain of official documents and
institutional discourse. Rather than relying solely on technical forensics or abstract legal argument, the
author reads statutes, directives, contracts, minutes, memoranda, e‑mails, and interrogation records as a
philosopher might read ordinary speech: with attention to what is said, what is presupposed, what is
omitted, and how meaning shifts across contexts.
This approach reveals patterns that a conventional investigation, constrained by organizational
hierarchies or limited mandates, is likely to miss. The evolution of the Defense Informatization
Directive, for example, is not treated merely as a sequence of administrative updates, but as a semantic
and normative trajectory that tracks how certain irregular practices were gradually insulated from
criticism. The On‑Nara approval chains are not seen as routine workflow artifacts, but as trace maps of
responsibility that show who knew what, when, and with what intent. The interrogation transcripts are
read not only for the answers they record, but for the implicit assumptions embedded in the questions
themselves.
The result is a form of evidence‑based discovery that is both analytic and practical. It demonstrates
that a single, well‑informed individual—armed with persistence, professional experience, and access to
records—can reconstruct a complex organizational crime that formal investigative bodies either failed
or refused to expose. It also suggests that language‑analytic methods can enrich the toolkit of auditors,
investigators, and scholars who seek to understand how institutions encode wrongdoing in seemingly
neutral texts.

sections.title: 5.2. Institutional Reform to Prevent Organizational Crime
5.2.1. Transparency in Defense Informatization and the Need to Break Cartels
sections.body:
The findings of this paper point to an urgent need for structural transparency in defense
informatization. When critical information systems are designed, operated, and evaluated within a
closed circle of agencies—such as DCIA, DIDC, user units, and affiliated research institutes—without
independent oversight, the temptation and the opportunity to manipulate technical and regulatory
narratives increase dramatically. The legacy KIATIS case illustrates how long‑term violations can be
maintained, even after a serious security breach, when those responsible for oversight are the same actors
who have reasons to prefer concealment.
Breaking this cycle requires more than formal rules. It demands institutional designs that separate
operational responsibility, regulatory authority, and independent review. For example, any future
amendment of core directives such as the Defense Informatization Directive should be subject to
external technical review and public justification, particularly when the amendments affect test and
evaluation procedures or security baselines. Similarly, research institutions like KIDA should not be
placed in a position where their commissioned work can be used to legitimize practices from which they
are institutionally dependent or in which they are materially implicated.

sections.title: 5.2.2. Protection of Whistleblowers and Safeguards Against Legal Weaponization
sections.body:
The seven‑layer system shows that the most powerful weapon against truth in this case was not only
technical manipulation, but the weaponization of legal and disciplinary tools. False internal
complaints, sham audits, and carefully framed fraud investigations can turn anti‑corruption mechanisms
into instruments of corruption. To counter this, robust protections for internal whistleblowers are
essential, especially in sectors where security considerations are frequently invoked to justify secrecy.
Such protections must go beyond formal declarations. They should include clear, enforceable rules
that prohibit retaliatory reassignment, isolation, reputational attacks, and pretextual investigations.
Independent channels must be available for officers and civil servants to report systemic issues without
having their disclosures redirected to the very actors they are accusing. Legal frameworks should
explicitly recognize that misuse of investigative powers to suppress disclosures is itself a serious
offense, on par with the underlying misconduct being concealed.

sections.title: 5.2.3. Reclaiming the Integrity of Military Justice and Oversight Institutions
sections.body:
The conduct of the Military Prosecutor’s Office, the internal audit bodies, and the civilian oversight
commissions in this case raises fundamental questions about institutional integrity. When investigative
and oversight institutions systematically fail to engage with evidence that challenges organizational
interests, they not only fail the individuals directly affected; they erode public trust in the rule of law.
Restoring integrity requires a combination of procedural reforms and cultural change.
Procedurally, there must be mechanisms to review and, where necessary, reopen cases in which new
evidence suggests that investigations were conducted under structural conflicts of interest. Decision
documents such as non‑prosecution statements should be subject to independent scrutiny when they are
alleged to rest on incomplete or biased readings of the record. Culturally, leaders within the military and
oversight bodies must signal that loyalty to the institution is not measured by the ability to suppress
uncomfortable truths, but by the willingness to confront them.

sections.title: 5.3. Broader Implications and Future Directions
5.3.1. A New Configuration of Evil in the Digital Age
sections.body:
The case analyzed in this paper indicates that organizational crime in the digital age has entered a new
phase. Concepts like APT, originally developed to describe external cyber threats, now help us
understand internal, human‑targeting operations conducted by the state against its own members.
These operations combine persistent, precise, and stealthy tactics across technical, legal, and social
domains.
In Arendt’s terms, we are moving from the “banality of evil” to a form of strategic, codified, and
data‑driven evil. The actors in this case were not simply unthinking functionaries swept along by a
pre‑existing machine. They actively designed systems, altered rules, commissioned research, and
weaponized legal procedures to construct a protective shell around a long‑standing security failure. Their
actions were not less dangerous because they were carried out with pens, keyboards, and procedural
language rather than with physical weapons.

sections.title: 5.3.2. The Role of Evidence, Memory, and Scholarship
sections.body:
Finally, this work underscores the importance of evidence and memory in resisting such
developments. The documents, logs, and transcripts analyzed here are more than technical records. They
are traces of choices—made, justified, and sometimes erased—that define the moral landscape of public
institutions. Preserving and interpreting these traces is a task not only for courts and prosecutors, but
also for scholars, journalists, and citizens.
The seven‑layer proof system is presented here as both a case study and a methodological proposal.
It suggests that complex organizational crimes can be uncovered through patient, layered reconstruction,
and that this reconstruction can, in turn, inform legal reform, policy design, and future research. If there
is a single lesson to carry forward, it is this: in a world where power increasingly operates through
systems, codes, and procedures, the defense of justice requires equally systematic, evidence‑based,
and intellectually honest inquiry.

sections.title: 5.3.3. The Task of Pursuing Legal Accountability
sections.body:
This study has sufficiently proven the existence of organized crime. What remains is the pursuit of
legal accountability. However, conventional investigative agencies face limitations, as the perpetrators
are directly connected to those very agencies. Extraordinary presidential measures are required. A truth
commission at the national level or the appointment of a special prosecutor must ensure an investigation
completely independent of existing investigative bodies. This case is not merely an individual incident
but reveals a fundamental problem within the national system, necessitating the President's direct
attention and action.
Utilizing existing institutions like the High-Ranking Public Officials Crime Investigation Office
(HPO) or the Board of Audit and Inspection could be considered. However, these bodies are ultimately
government agencies, making it difficult to guarantee complete independence. Instead, a special
investigation team composed of experts on this case is needed to immediately conduct searches and
seizures and confirm criminal facts through investigation.
An international approach is also necessary. This case is a crucial example demonstrating how
organized crime is evolving in modern democratic societies. Attention from international organizations
like the UN Human Rights Committee or Transparency International can complement the limitations of
domestic solutions.
In conclusion, this study has thoroughly proven the reality of unprecedented, organized crime through
a seven-layered verification system based on evidence-based discovery methodology. What is now
required is substantive change based on these findings. It is our duty to prevent the recurrence of such
crimes by strengthening transparency, improving audit systems, and establishing personal protection
mechanisms. Above all, it is urgent to pursue legal accountability through immediate investigations to
achieve justice. We hope this research, which began with the belief that "the truth will inevitably emerge,
even if it takes time," will lead to genuine change in Korean society.

sections.title: 5.3.4. To the authorities, and to the individual
sections.body:
This author appeals to the authorities of the Republic of Korea and the state institutions representing
the power of its citizens. This paper addresses the unavoidable, unforeseen events that befell me—a
soldier who faithfully served his duty for 32 years—perpetrated by the military prosecution and the
Ministry of National Defense's manufacturing division through a long-term, organized, and collective
conspiracy, concealment, fabrication, concealment, human rights violations, and criminal stigmatization
perpetrated by the defense information cartel within the Military Prosecutor's Office and the Ministry of
National Defense. It is an analytical report proven through the 7-Layer Verification System based on
13,528 pages of evidence accumulated over four years.
This is a product born from digital evidence, discovered and unraveled by my own volition as an
unintended 'data detective' who transformed to confront this injustice and uncover its true nature.
Crucially, this case involves spaces that must be managed under the National Contract Act, and the
actions performed within them incorporate information technology (IT), enabling the accountability
demanded by audits. Therefore, it is a rare outcome in modern Korean history where the prosecution's
criminal techniques and methods can be traced based on evidence captured digitally in real-time. I
discovered this by employing every method I learned and mastered to uncover the essence behind the
scheme that made me a criminal. Living with shame and humiliation is harder for me than death itself.
This is the product of my resolve, honing the blade of determination at death's door and persevering to
solve the problem.

Furthermore, I wish you to note that I recognized this investigation was related to the "2016 North
Korean Hacking of the Defense Integrated Data Center." On September 2022, I sent a detailed
booklet and petition proving this fact through rigorous argumentation to the Minister of National
Defense (and Military Advisor), the Chief of the Military Prosecutors' Office and its organization, the
Military Prosecutors' Office, and the prosecutor and investigators handling my case.55 I had hoped for
the military's self-purification (Records pp. 4,979 ff., pp. 5,578 ff., pp. 5,671 ff.).
However, despite the systematic and collective conspiracy, concealment, manipulation, cover-up,
human rights violations, and criminal stigmatization, and despite the reality of my own powerlessness
and the destruction of my family, the military organization I served loyally for 32 years as a soldier,
along with its leadership and the cartel-like group I once considered colleagues, remained fixated until
the very end on branding me a criminal. They mobilized all their power to do so, exploiting my pure
loyalty. Even now, that organization, which has dominated most of my life, saw me not only as someone
pure enough to be exploited and weak enough to be discarded, but also as mere prey—a sacrificial lamb
for the first-ever <APT-style human targeting> in human history. Just like the scenes of evil witnessed
in the so-called "modern-day Japanese Unit 731 biological experiments," I was nothing more than a
target.
For the organization and the cartel, they had built, it was likely nothing more than a game they could
fully enjoy, utilizing their well-established network and conditions to execute without hesitation, all in
the name of preserving their interests and themselves. To that group, it was undoubtedly a wellcalculated, confident operation with favorable odds, one that remained undetected.
Therefore, the nation's leadership and power institutions must first, regarding this matter, does not
refrain from questioning the sincerity of the individuals involved—including myself, the military
prosecution team, and defense manufacturing officials—during detention requests, searches and
seizures, and investigations. Instead, they must ask whether the actions of those involved in this project
comply with directives and regulations and are reasonable. They must also logically examine whether

55 That's unacceptable. I requested assistance from the Anti-Corruption and Civil Rights Commission and the National Human
Rights Commission, submitting thousands of pages of evidence. Yet, not only did they refuse to even call me as a witness,
but I was told—I couldn't believe my ears—that they would openly hand over my materials to the Ministry of National
Defense. I request you listen to the recording I provided (over 30 minutes). Beyond shock, it makes me question whether
the place I'm living in is reality, or even the Republic of Korea.
those actions ultimately led to the project's success or failure. Furthermore, while verifying the evidence
and proof presented, if the military prosecution, aiming for indictment or to cover up their own or related
organizations' mistakes, uses the power of prosecutorial authority to unjustly brand someone a criminal
to the end through fabrication and distortion, they must be held criminally responsible. I earnestly
request that the military prosecutors and the defense intelligence cartel mob, who destroyed a person's
life and family's well-being, be thoroughly "severed" according to their crimes, through the legal
procedures of the state power granted by the people. The methods employed and the cruelty involved
transcend human behavior. I hope they receive a punishment commensurate with the gravity of their
actions.
I have come to realize anew that I have lived my life thus far viewing everyone I have encountered
through a lens of goodwill. Though this has caused me much harm, I have strived to live as kindly as
possible within my circumstances, seeking to cause no harm to others. However, due to this irrational
and absurd criminal act by state power institutions, I doubt I can return to the life I once lived, viewing
others through that same lens. The crucial truth I grasped after four years of grappling with this issue is
this: "When I viewed their behavior through a lens of goodwill, the problem remained unsolved. But
once I recognized their perspective—that 'evil gaze'—I could finally approach the essence of this
matter." It pains me deeply to realize that the "knowing the enemy" principle learned from the art of war
can be applied to those I once considered comrades. Yet now, I must cultivate the strength to accept this
pain, confront and forgive my own previously unrecognized weaknesses and vulnerabilities, and hope
to gain the wisdom to build resilience.
Finally, I implore you!
The easiest way to understand the defense informatization cartel can be found in the statements made
by the military prosecutor.56 Immediate and lightning-fast follow-up actions, the restoration of my honor,
and the dismantling of the defense informatization cartel
56 This is what a military prosecutor told me on October 2022: "We summoned and investigated all relevant individuals.
We conducted an enormous number of witness interviews. We called in virtually everyone connected to the case. (Record
p. 11,162)." These individuals are either the ringleaders of the defense IT cartel or part of its supporting forces. Verifying
the statements of those summoned as witnesses in the investigation conducted by the military prosecution team is the key
to identifying the cartel members.

After serving as a U.S. military staff officer at CJTF-7 Headquarters in Iraq in 2008, I proposed a solution to
this problem in my return report (August 2004, Record p. 4670), but it was deleted. The common pattern in
Sun Tzu's Art of War and the wars waged by the United States is 'war by deployment', meaning war fought
on foreign soil. While the U.S. deploys and utilizes its own data as war intelligence, we are not deploying—
that location is the battlefield itself. It is an environment where data cannot survive. A COOP plan
(Contingency Plan, Continuity of Operation Plan) that can be entrusted to 'Samsung' and other South Korean
private companies in a second region, not our own country, must be established as reality as soon as possible.
What was difficult in the past is no longer difficult now. We need only benchmark the concept of entrusting
physical space, as seen with Amazon and Microsoft. Only then can the environmental conditions for our
nation to win in war be prepared, and only then can the conditions for our nation to prevail on future
battlefields be formed.57 , I understand that the protection of citizens' lives and property during wartime is
heavily influenced by the power generated by indirect defense laws. Consider this: the Pyeongtaek US
military base and the Combined Forces Command are created and operated in the same way as building a
virtual tunnel to California, USA, based on physical geographical location. A US VLAN was also created in
Afghanistan. If a physical VLAN can be created on vast land like the Pyeongtaek US military base, why is it
difficult to create a similar physical VLAN with countries that are allies of Korea during peacetime and have
Korea's leading companies? This is a matter of national survival. The author still thinks the following about
this. It was right then, and it's right now! (Search date: 2020. 9.27).

Even now, 20 years later, it hasn't been done. Why? We have no intention of doing so. The reason is likely
dependence on foreign powers for matters of national importance. The painful lesson learned from 32 years of
military service seems to be that <we do not truly understand the real meaning of Supported and Supporting,
Dependence and Independence, Interdependence>.

<ABSTRACT>

Beyond Cybersecurity
APT-Style Individual Targeted Attacks by ROK MND's Defense Department Agencies and
Affiliated Organizations (2017.8.7~2022.10.11)
Lee Young Ju
This treatise reveals that agencies including mainly the Ministry of National Defense Military
Prosecutor's Office, Ministry of National Defense intelligence agencies, Defense Intelligence
Command, Cyber Operations Command, and Korea Institute for Defense Analyses (KIDA) conspired
to conceal the root server of the 2016 North Korean hacking incident. To cover their mistakes and
deceive the public and media with falsehoods, they employed sophisticated APT-style hacking
techniques over an extended period. Targeting a military officer with 32 years of service, they
systematically fabricated and manipulated evidence through assigned roles, concealed the truth,
committed unethical human rights violations, reported abuse of power, and carried out character
assassination by branding him mentally ill. They then forced his dishonorable discharge by falsely
labeling him a criminal for absurd reasons, thereby concealing national failure, preserving their own
interests, and protecting the organization. Furthermore, they fundamentally obstructed accountability by
dispersing the roles of each stakeholder involved in the 2016 hacking incident. This is an incomparably
heinous crime on a different level from the crime scene and its 'banal evil' described in Hannah Arendt's
Eichmann in Jerusalem. This represents a new form of event distinct from the crime scene of 'the banality
of evil' during World War II. It is likely the first highly sophisticated and precise case in human history
where a specialized organization of information technology experts in the digital age utilized the
cybersecurity techniques, they had learned to hunt down a single individual in a many-to-one structure,
targeting them relentlessly until their desired objective was achieved. This study— —is an argument
forged from the findings of a grueling four-year struggle. It is based on data obtained from the scene of
long-term manipulation and conspiracy, national contract law, related project documents, defense
guidelines and regulations, manuals, official government documents, and statements from relevant
parties, as the subject of APT-style human targeting. This treatise is a thesis proven by a seven-layered
proof system. It is built upon this evidence base, accumulating experience to uncover the threads of core
issues and the intentions and traces of the cartels. It integrates these discoveries across layers to extract
insights through a method of discovery.
Keywords: Defense Integrated Data Center (DIDC), Center 1 (Yongin)/Center 2 (Gyeryongdae),
Development Test Evaluation, Project Management Agency, Project Lead Agency, Project Control
Agency, Operational Test Evaluation, Active-X, 7-Layer Proof System, 2016 DIDC North Korean
Hacking, KIATIS (Korean War Casualties Integrated Information System).
